[[ja:鳥取造]] [[ja:鳥取部]] [[ja:鳥養部]] [[ja:%E9%B3%A5%E9%A4%8A%E9%83%A8]] [[sn:Torikai-be]] [[sn:orikai-be]]
<!--Torikai-be--> <!--Torikai-be-->

'''Torikaibe''' (鳥養部) was a professional group (品部) in Japan before the [[Taika Reform]] that was responsible for raising and breeding birds captured by the [[Toribe]] (鳥取部) at the imperial court's request. It was also written as "Torigai-be" or "Toriame-be" (鳥飼部・鳥甘部). This article also covers the [[Toribe]] (ととりべ), another occupational group that specialized in capturing waterfowl for the court.
== Overview (概要) ==
The '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) oversaw the '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部), a specialized occupational group (*be*) responsible for capturing ornamental waterfowl. Meanwhile, the '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部) was likely responsible for the care and breeding of these captured birds.
In the central administration, high-Ranking {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造|12=draft|12=draft}} were given the title of '''"Zō"''' (造), later changed to '''"Muraji"''' (連), while lower-Ranking officials held titles such as '''"Omi"''' (臣), '''"Atonae"''' (直), '''"Zō"''' (造), and '''"Kubi"''' (首).<ref>『日本書紀』(二)補注p349、岩波文庫、1994th year </ref>
The descendants of {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河桁命|12=draft|12=draft}} were known as '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}} and '''Totori Muraji''' (鳥取連) in the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|12=draft|12=draft}}. His son, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|12=draft|12=draft}}, was also identified as the ancestor of the '''Totori Muraji''' in the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀|12=draft|12=draft}}.
The '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部) is documented in historical records from {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Bitchū Province|ja|備中国|12=draft|12=draft}}. The name also appears in the form of '''villages, postal relay stations (eki), and shrines''' in various regions, including {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Izumi Province|ja|和泉国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|12=draft|12=draft}}.
The '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部, also written 鳥甘部) appears exclusively in historical records from {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft|12=draft}}. Based on place names, they were likely concentrated in the '''Karumura and Iware''' (磐余) regions, with additional settlements in {{ill|Settsu Province|ja|摂津国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑前国|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国|12=draft|12=draft}}, where there were villages and districts named '''Torikabe''' (鳥養村, 鳥養郷).
== Origins (由来) ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft|12=draft}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi|ja|誉津別命|12=draft|12=draft}} (ほむちわけ の おおきみ) did not speak for a long time even after reaching adulthood, possibly due to the shock of his mother’s death, {{ill|Saho-hime|ja|狭穂姫命|lt=Saho-hime no Mikoto|12=draft|12=draft}}. One day, while on a boat excursion, he heard the voice of a {{ill|Crested ibis|ja|鵠|12=draft|12=draft}} (くぐい) flying high in the sky and moved his jaw, uttering his first words.
His father, the emperor, dispatched {{ill|Yamanobe no Ōtaka|ja|山辺大鶙|12=draft|12=draft}} to chase the ibis across {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Harima Province|ja|播磨国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tajima Province|ja|但馬国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国|12=draft|12=draft}}, until finally capturing it at Wanaumi no Minato (和那美之水門) in the {{ill|Koshi region|ja|越国|12=draft|12=draft}} using a trap net. However, the prince still could not speak.
Later, in a dream, the emperor was visited by {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=draft|12=draft}}, who instructed him to build a shrine. It was decided that Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi should make a pilgrimage to {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社|12=draft|12=draft}}, with his attendant selected by divination. {{ill|Aketatsu no Ōkimi|ja|曙立王|12=draft|12=draft}} was chosen and, to prove the divine message, he performed a {{ill|ukei|ja|うけい|12=draft|12=draft}} by reviving a {{ill|heron|ja|鷺|12=draft|12=draft}} and an {{ill|evergreen oak|ja|樫|12=draft|12=draft}} tree.
Emperor Suinin, convinced, sent Aketatsu no Ōkimi along with his younger brother, {{ill|Unakami no Ōkimi|ja|菟上王|12=draft|12=draft}}, to accompany the prince to Izumo. There, the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=draft|12=draft}} prepared a temporary shrine decorated with fresh green leaves and constructed an artificial hill. As they prepared to dine by the lower reaches of the {{ill|Hii River|ja|斐伊川|12=draft|12=draft}}, the prince finally spoke, asking about the artificial hill. This event led to the establishment of the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Torikamebe|ja|鳥甘部|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Homujibe|ja|品遅部|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōyu-e|ja|大湯坐|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Wakayu-e|ja|若湯坐|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>『古事記』中巻、垂仁天皇条より</ref>
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Prince Homutsuwake|ja|誉津別皇子|12=draft|12=draft}}, son of Emperor Suinin, did not speak even after growing a beard and continued to cry like a child. One day, he saw a crested ibis and uttered his first words: "What is that?" The emperor then sent {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawatana|ja|天湯河板挙|12=draft|12=draft}} to chase the ibis to Izumo (or Tajima, according to another account), where he captured it. As a result, the prince was able to speak.
The emperor then granted Ame-no-Yukawatana the surname "Tottori no Miyatsuko" (鳥取造), meaning "head of the bird-catchers," and established the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Toriyaube|ja|鳥養部|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Homutsube|ja|誉津部|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>『日本書紀』垂仁天皇23rd year 10月8日条、11月2日条</ref>
{{ill|Hōga Toshio|ja|宝賀寿男|12=draft|12=draft}} suggests that, like the {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|12=draft|12=draft}} (horse breeders) and the {{ill|Ikaibe|ja|猪養部|12=draft|12=draft}} (boar hunters), members of the Toriyaube (鳥養部) likely bore facial tattoos (黥, mesaki) as part of their occupation. Although the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}} mentions punitive tattoos, he argues that tattooing originally served a protective, magical purpose against animal attacks. He further suggests that the punitive interpretation arose later, when tattooing became associated with criminal punishment.<ref>「[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/hitori/irezumi.htm 入墨についての雑考]」『古樹紀之房間』、2010th year 。</ref>
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Musa no Suguri Ao|ja|身狭村主青|12=draft|12=draft}} and his men transported imported {{ill|geese|ja|ガチョウ|12=draft|12=draft}} from the {{ill|Southern Dynasties (China)|ja|南朝|12=draft|12=draft}} to {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国|12=draft|12=draft}}. However, a dog belonging to either {{ill|Minuma no Kimi|ja|水沼県主|12=draft|12=draft}} or, in another account, {{ill|Mine no Agatanushi Numaro|ja|嶺県主泥麻呂|12=draft|12=draft}}, killed one of the birds. In a panic, the dog’s owner offered ten white swans and several "Toriyaubito" (養鳥人, birdkeepers) as compensation.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 9月4日条</ref>
The emperor settled these birdkeepers in Karumura and Iwaremura.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 10月7日条</ref>
Later, when a bird raised by the "Tori no Tsukasa" (鳥官, the imperial birdkeepers) was killed by a pet dog in {{ill|Uda District|ja|宇陀|12=draft|12=draft}}, the emperor punished the dog’s owner by tattooing his face and assigning him to the Toriyaube. The same punishment was imposed on men from {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|12=draft|12=draft}} who were serving in forced labor and had spoken out against the emperor’s decision.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇11th year 10月条</ref>
{{ill|Tanigawa Ken'ichi|ja|谷川健一|12=draft|12=draft}} has noted a deep connection between metal refining and bird-related myths. He argues that the name "Homutsuwake" implies a birth in fire and that "Yue" (湯坐) secretly carries the meaning of "molten metal." He also points to links between {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawatana|ja|天湯河板挙|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|12=draft|12=draft}}, suggesting that the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, who worshipped the thunder god {{ill|Nigi-hayahinomikoto|ja|ニギハヤヒ|12=draft|12=draft}}, may have overseen the Toriyaube.<ref>『白鳥伝説』(上)p215 - p238、集英社文庫</ref>
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}} also records the story of {{ill|Totori-be no Yorozu|ja|捕鳥部万|12=draft|12=draft}}, a retainer of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, who defended the clan’s estate during the {{ill|Battle against Mononobe no Moriya|ja|物部守屋|12=draft|12=draft}} in the second year of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇|12=draft|12=draft}} (587). After their defeat, he fled but resisted capture by the imperial forces.<ref>『日本書紀』{{ill|Emperor Sushun|ja|崇峻天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}即位前紀（用明天皇2nd year 7月条）</ref>

According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft|12=draft}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi|ja|誉津別命|12=draft|12=draft}} (ほむちわけ の おおきみ) did not speak for a long time even after reaching adulthood, possibly due to the shock of his mother’s death, {{ill|Saho-hime|ja|狭穂姫命|lt=Saho-hime no Mikoto|12=draft|12=draft}}. One day, while on a boat excursion, he heard the voice of a {{ill|Crested ibis|ja|鵠|12=draft|12=draft}} (くぐい) flying high in the sky and moved his jaw, uttering his first words.
His father, the emperor, dispatched {{ill|Yamanobe no Ōtaka|ja|山辺大鶙|12=draft|12=draft}} to chase the ibis across {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Harima Province|ja|播磨国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tajima Province|ja|但馬国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国|12=draft|12=draft}}, until finally capturing it at Wanaumi no Minato (和那美之水門) in the {{ill|Koshi region|ja|越国|12=draft|12=draft}} using a trap net. However, the prince still could not speak.
Later, in a dream, the emperor was visited by {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=draft|12=draft}}, who instructed him to build a shrine. It was decided that Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi should make a pilgrimage to {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社|12=draft|12=draft}}, with his attendant selected by divination. {{ill|Aketatsu no Ōkimi|ja|曙立王|12=draft|12=draft}} was chosen and, to prove the divine message, he performed a {{ill|ukei|ja|うけい|12=draft|12=draft}} by reviving a {{ill|heron|ja|鷺|12=draft|12=draft}} and an {{ill|evergreen oak|ja|樫|12=draft|12=draft}} tree.
Emperor Suinin, convinced, sent Aketatsu no Ōkimi along with his younger brother, {{ill|Unakami no Ōkimi|ja|菟上王|12=draft|12=draft}}, to accompany the prince to Izumo. There, the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=draft|12=draft}} prepared a temporary shrine decorated with fresh green leaves and constructed an artificial hill. As they prepared to dine by the lower reaches of the {{ill|Hii River|ja|斐伊川|12=draft|12=draft}}, the prince finally spoke, asking about the artificial hill. This event led to the establishment of the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Torikamebe|ja|鳥甘部|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Homujibe|ja|品遅部|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōyu-e|ja|大湯坐|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Wakayu-e|ja|若湯坐|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>『古事記』中巻、垂仁天皇条より</ref>
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Prince Homutsuwake|ja|誉津別皇子|12=draft|12=draft}}, son of Emperor Suinin, did not speak even after growing a beard and continued to cry like a child. One day, he saw a crested ibis and uttered his first words: "What is that?" The emperor then sent {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawatana|ja|天湯河板挙|12=draft|12=draft}} to chase the ibis to Izumo (or Tajima, according to another account), where he captured it. As a result, the prince was able to speak.
The emperor then granted Ame-no-Yukawatana the surname "Tottori no Miyatsuko" (鳥取造), meaning "head of the bird-catchers," and established the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Toriyaube|ja|鳥養部|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Homutsube|ja|誉津部|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>『日本書紀』垂仁天皇23rd year 10月8日条、11月2日条</ref>
{{ill|Hōga Toshio|ja|宝賀寿男|12=draft|12=draft}} suggests that, like the {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|12=draft|12=draft}} (horse breeders) and the {{ill|Ikaibe|ja|猪養部|12=draft|12=draft}} (boar hunters), members of the Toriyaube (鳥養部) likely bore facial tattoos (黥, mesaki) as part of their occupation. Although the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}} mentions punitive tattoos, he argues that tattooing originally served a protective, magical purpose against animal attacks. He further suggests that the punitive interpretation arose later, when tattooing became associated with criminal punishment.<ref>「[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/hitori/irezumi.htm 入墨についての雑考]」『古樹紀之房間』、2010th year 。</ref>
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Musa no Suguri Ao|ja|身狭村主青|12=draft|12=draft}} and his men transported imported {{ill|geese|ja|ガチョウ|12=draft|12=draft}} from the {{ill|Southern Dynasties (China)|ja|南朝|12=draft|12=draft}} to {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国|12=draft|12=draft}}. However, a dog belonging to either {{ill|Minuma no Kimi|ja|水沼県主|12=draft|12=draft}} or, in another account, {{ill|Mine no Agatanushi Numaro|ja|嶺県主泥麻呂|12=draft|12=draft}}, killed one of the birds. In a panic, the dog’s owner offered ten white swans and several "Toriyaubito" (養鳥人, birdkeepers) as compensation.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 9月4日条</ref>
The emperor settled these birdkeepers in Karumura and Iwaremura.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 10月7日条</ref>
Later, when a bird raised by the "Tori no Tsukasa" (鳥官, the imperial birdkeepers) was killed by a pet dog in {{ill|Uda District|ja|宇陀|12=draft|12=draft}}, the emperor punished the dog’s owner by tattooing his face and assigning him to the Toriyaube. The same punishment was imposed on men from {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|12=draft|12=draft}} who were serving in forced labor and had spoken out against the emperor’s decision.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇11th year 10月条</ref>
{{ill|Tanigawa Ken'ichi|ja|谷川健一|12=draft|12=draft}} has noted a deep connection between metal refining and bird-related myths. He argues that the name "Homutsuwake" implies a birth in fire and that "Yue" (湯坐) secretly carries the meaning of "molten metal." He also points to links between {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawatana|ja|天湯河板挙|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|12=draft|12=draft}}, suggesting that the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, who worshipped the thunder god {{ill|Nigi-hayahinomikoto|ja|ニギハヤヒ|12=draft|12=draft}}, may have overseen the Toriyaube.<ref>『白鳥伝説』(上)p215 - p238、集英社文庫</ref>
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}} also records the story of {{ill|Totori-be no Yorozu|ja|捕鳥部万|12=draft|12=draft}}, a retainer of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, who defended the clan’s estate during the {{ill|Battle against Mononobe no Moriya|ja|物部守屋|12=draft|12=draft}} in the second year of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇|12=draft|12=draft}} (587). After their defeat, he fled but resisted capture by the imperial forces.<ref>『日本書紀』{{ill|Emperor Sushun|ja|崇峻天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}即位前紀（用明天皇2nd year 7月条）</ref>

[[ja:鳥取造]] [[ja:鳥取部]] [[ja:鳥養部]] [[ja:%E9%B3%A5%E9%A4%8A%E9%83%A8]] [[sn:Torikai-be]] [[sn:orikai-be]]
<!--Torikai-be--> <!--Torikai-be-->

'''Torikaibe''' (鳥養部) was a professional group (品部) in Japan before the [[Taika Reform]] that was responsible for raising and breeding birds captured by the [[Toribe]] (鳥取部) at the imperial court's request. It was also written as "Torigai-be" or "Toriame-be" (鳥飼部・鳥甘部). This article also covers the [[Toribe]] (ととりべ), another occupational group that specialized in capturing waterfowl for the court.
== Overview (概要) ==
The '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) oversaw the '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部), a specialized occupational group (*be*) responsible for capturing ornamental waterfowl. Meanwhile, the '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部) was likely responsible for the care and breeding of these captured birds.
In the central administration, high-Ranking {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造|12=draft|12=draft}} were given the title of '''"Zō"''' (造), later changed to '''"Muraji"''' (連), while lower-Ranking officials held titles such as '''"Omi"''' (臣), '''"Atonae"''' (直), '''"Zō"''' (造), and '''"Kubi"''' (首).<ref>『日本書紀』(二)補注p349、岩波文庫、1994th year </ref>
The descendants of {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河桁命|12=draft|12=draft}} were known as '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}} and '''Totori Muraji''' (鳥取連) in the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|12=draft|12=draft}}. His son, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|12=draft|12=draft}}, was also identified as the ancestor of the '''Totori Muraji''' in the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀|12=draft|12=draft}}.
The '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部) is documented in historical records from {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Bitchū Province|ja|備中国|12=draft|12=draft}}. The name also appears in the form of '''villages, postal relay stations (eki), and shrines''' in various regions, including {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Izumi Province|ja|和泉国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|12=draft|12=draft}}.
The '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部, also written 鳥甘部) appears exclusively in historical records from {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft|12=draft}}. Based on place names, they were likely concentrated in the '''Karumura and Iware''' (磐余) regions, with additional settlements in {{ill|Settsu Province|ja|摂津国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑前国|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国|12=draft|12=draft}}, where there were villages and districts named '''Torikabe''' (鳥養村, 鳥養郷).
== Origins (由来) ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft|12=draft}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi|ja|誉津別命|12=draft|12=draft}} (ほむちわけ の おおきみ) did not speak for a long time even after reaching adulthood, possibly due to the shock of his mother’s death, {{ill|Saho-hime|ja|狭穂姫命|lt=Saho-hime no Mikoto|12=draft|12=draft}}. However, the prince still could not speak.
Later, in a dream, the emperor was visited by {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=draft|12=draft}}, who instructed him to build a shrine. It was decided that Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi should make a pilgrimage to {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社|12=draft|12=draft}}, with his attendant selected by divination. {{ill|Aketatsu no Ōkimi|ja|曙立王|12=draft|12=draft}} was chosen and, to prove the divine message, he performed a {{ill|ukei|ja|うけい|12=draft|12=draft}} by reviving a {{ill|heron|ja|鷺|12=draft|12=draft}} and an {{ill|evergreen oak|ja|樫|12=draft|12=draft}} tree.
Emperor Suinin, convinced, sent Aketatsu no Ōkimi along with his younger brother, {{ill|Unakami no Ōkimi|ja|菟上王|12=draft|12=draft}}, to accompany the prince to Izumo. There, the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=draft|12=draft}} prepared a temporary shrine decorated with fresh green leaves and constructed an artificial hill. As they prepared to dine by the lower reaches of the {{ill|Hii River|ja|斐伊川|12=draft|12=draft}}, the prince finally spoke, asking about the artificial hill. This event led to the establishment of the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Torikamebe|ja|鳥甘部|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Homujibe|ja|品遅部|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōyu-e|ja|大湯坐|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Wakayu-e|ja|若湯坐|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>『古事記』中巻、垂仁天皇条より</ref>
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Prince Homutsuwake|ja|誉津別皇子|12=draft|12=draft}}, son of Emperor Suinin, did not speak even after growing a beard and continued to cry like a child. One day, he saw a crested ibis and uttered his first words: "What is that?" The emperor then sent {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawatana|ja|天湯河板挙|12=draft|12=draft}} to chase the ibis to Izumo (or Tajima, according to another account), where he captured it. As a result, the prince was able to speak.
The emperor then granted Ame-no-Yukawatana the surname "Tottori no Miyatsuko" (鳥取造), meaning "head of the bird-catchers," and established the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Toriyaube|ja|鳥養部|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Homutsube|ja|誉津部|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>『日本書紀』垂仁天皇23rd year 10月8日条、11月2日条</ref>
{{ill|Hōga Toshio|ja|宝賀寿男|12=draft|12=draft}} suggests that, like the {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|12=draft|12=draft}} (horse breeders) and the {{ill|Ikaibe|ja|猪養部|12=draft|12=draft}} (boar hunters), members of the Toriyaube (鳥養部) likely bore facial tattoos (黥, mesaki) as part of their occupation. '''Torikaibe''' (鳥養部) was a professional group (品部) in Japan before the [[Taika Reform]] that was responsible for raising and breeding birds captured by the [[Toribe]] (鳥取部) at the imperial court's request. It was also written as "Torigai-be" or "Toriame-be" (鳥飼部・鳥甘部). This article also covers the [[Toribe]] (ととりべ), another occupational group that specialized in capturing waterfowl for the court.
== Overview (概要) ==
The '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) oversaw the '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部), a specialized occupational group (*be*) responsible for capturing ornamental waterfowl. Meanwhile, the '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部) was likely responsible for the care and breeding of these captured birds.
In the central administration, high-Ranking {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were given the title of '''"Zō"''' (造), later changed to '''"Muraji"''' (連), while lower-Ranking officials held titles such as '''"Omi"''' (臣), '''"Atonae"''' (直), '''"Zō"''' (造), and '''"Kubi"''' (首).<ref>『日本書紀』(二)補注p349、岩波文庫、1994th year </ref>
The descendants of {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河桁命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were known as '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and '''Totori Muraji''' (鳥取連) in the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. His son, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, was also identified as the ancestor of the '''Totori Muraji''' in the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
The '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部) is documented in historical records from {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Bitchū Province|ja|備中国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The name also appears in the form of '''villages, postal relay stations (eki), and shrines''' in various regions, including {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Izumi Province|ja|和泉国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
The '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部, also written 鳥甘部) appears exclusively in historical records from {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Based on place names, they were likely concentrated in the '''Karumura and Iware''' (磐余) regions, with additional settlements in {{ill|Settsu Province|ja|摂津国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑前国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where there were villages and districts named '''Torikabe''' (鳥養村, 鳥養郷).
== Origins (由来) ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi|ja|誉津別命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (ほむちわけ の おおきみ) did not speak for a long time even after reaching adulthood, possibly due to the shock of his mother’s death, {{ill|Saho-hime|ja|狭穂姫命|lt=Saho-hime no Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. One day, while on a boat excursion, he heard the voice of a {{ill|Crested ibis|ja|鵠|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (くぐい) flying high in the sky and moved his jaw, uttering his first words.
His father, the emperor, dispatched {{ill|Yamanobe no Ōtaka|ja|山辺大鶙|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to chase the ibis across {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Harima Province|ja|播磨国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tajima Province|ja|但馬国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, until finally capturing it at Wanaumi no Minato (和那美之水門) in the {{ill|Koshi region|ja|越国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} using a trap net. However, the prince still could not speak.
Later, in a dream, the emperor was visited by {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who instructed him to build a shrine. It was decided that Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi should make a pilgrimage to {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with his attendant selected by divination. {{ill|Aketatsu no Ōkimi|ja|曙立王|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was chosen and, to prove the divine message, he performed a {{ill|ukei|ja|うけい|qq=draft|qq=draft}} by reviving a {{ill|heron|ja|鷺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and an {{ill|evergreen oak|ja|樫|qq=draft|qq=draft}} tree.
Emperor Suinin, convinced, sent Aketatsu no Ōkimi along with his younger brother, {{ill|Unakami no Ōkimi|ja|菟上王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, to accompany the prince to Izumo. There, the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} prepared a temporary shrine decorated with fresh green leaves and constructed an artificial hill. As they prepared to dine by the lower reaches of the {{ill|Hii River|ja|斐伊川|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the prince finally spoke, asking about the artificial hill. This event led to the establishment of the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Torikamebe|ja|鳥甘部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Homujibe|ja|品遅部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōyu-e|ja|大湯坐|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Wakayu-e|ja|若湯坐|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>『古事記』中巻、垂仁天皇条より</ref>
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Prince Homutsuwake|ja|誉津別皇子|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, son of Emperor Suinin, did not speak even after growing a beard and continued to cry like a child. One day, he saw a crested ibis and uttered his first words: "What is that?" The emperor then sent {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawatana|ja|天湯河板挙|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to chase the ibis to Izumo (or Tajima, according to another account), where he captured it. As a result, the prince was able to speak.
The emperor then granted Ame-no-Yukawatana the surname "Tottori no Miyatsuko" (鳥取造), meaning "head of the bird-catchers," and established the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Toriyaube|ja|鳥養部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Homutsube|ja|誉津部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>『日本書紀』垂仁天皇23rd year 10月8日条、11月2日条</ref>
{{ill|Hōga Toshio|ja|宝賀寿男|qq=draft|qq=draft}} suggests that, like the {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (horse breeders) and the {{ill|Ikaibe|ja|猪養部|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (boar hunters), members of the Toriyaube (鳥養部) likely bore facial tattoos (黥, mesaki) as part of their occupation. Although the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} mentions punitive tattoos, he argues that tattooing originally served a protective, magical purpose against animal attacks. He further suggests that the punitive interpretation arose later, when tattooing became associated with criminal punishment.<ref>「[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/hitori/irezumi.htm 入墨についての雑考]」『古樹紀之房間』、2010th year 。</ref>
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Musa no Suguri Ao|ja|身狭村主青|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and his men transported imported {{ill|geese|ja|ガチョウ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from the {{ill|Southern Dynasties (China)|ja|南朝|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, a dog belonging to either {{ill|Minuma no Kimi|ja|水沼県主|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or, in another account, {{ill|Mine no Agatanushi Numaro|ja|嶺県主泥麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, killed one of the birds. In a panic, the dog’s owner offered ten white swans and several "Toriyaubito" (養鳥人, birdkeepers) as compensation.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 9月4日条</ref>
The emperor settled these birdkeepers in Karumura and Iwaremura.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 10月7日条</ref>
Later, when a bird raised by the "Tori no Tsukasa" (鳥官, the imperial birdkeepers) was killed by a pet dog in {{ill|Uda District|ja|宇陀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the emperor punished the dog’s owner by tattooing his face and assigning him to the Toriyaube. The same punishment was imposed on men from {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} who were serving in forced labor and had spoken out against the emperor’s decision.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇11th year 10月条</ref>
{{ill|Tanigawa Ken'ichi|ja|谷川健一|qq=draft|qq=draft}} has noted a deep connection between metal refining and bird-related myths. He argues that the name "Homutsuwake" implies a birth in fire and that "Yue" (湯坐) secretly carries the meaning of "molten metal." He also points to links between {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawatana|ja|天湯河板挙|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, suggesting that the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who worshipped the thunder god {{ill|Nigi-hayahinomikoto|ja|ニギハヤヒ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, may have overseen the Toriyaube.<ref>『白鳥伝説』(上)p215 - p238、集英社文庫</ref>
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} also records the story of {{ill|Totori-be no Yorozu|ja|捕鳥部万|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a retainer of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who defended the clan’s estate during the {{ill|Battle against Mononobe no Moriya|ja|物部守屋|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the second year of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (587). After their defeat, he fled but resisted capture by the imperial forces.<ref>『日本書紀』{{ill|Emperor Sushun|ja|崇峻天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}即位前紀（用明天皇2nd year 7月条）</ref>

<!--Torikai-be--> <!--Torikai-be-->

'''Torikaibe''' (鳥養部) was a professional group (品部) in Japan before the [[Taika Reform]] that was responsible for raising and breeding birds captured by the [[Toribe]] (鳥取部) at the imperial court's request. It was also written as "Torigai-be" or "Toriame-be" (鳥飼部・鳥甘部). This article also covers the [[Toribe]] (ととりべ), another occupational group that specialized in capturing waterfowl for the court.
== Overview (概要) ==
The '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) oversaw the '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部), a specialized occupational group (*be*) responsible for capturing ornamental waterfowl. Meanwhile, the '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部) was likely responsible for the care and breeding of these captured birds.
In the central administration, high-Ranking {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were given the title of '''"Zō"''' (造), later changed to '''"Muraji"''' (連), while lower-Ranking officials held titles such as '''"Omi"''' (臣), '''"Atonae"''' (直), '''"Zō"''' (造), and '''"Kubi"''' (首).<ref>『日本書紀』(二)補注p349、岩波文庫、1994th year </ref>
The descendants of {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河桁命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were known as '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and '''Totori Muraji''' (鳥取連) in the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. His son, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, was also identified as the ancestor of the '''Totori Muraji''' in the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
The '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部) is documented in historical records from {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Bitchū Province|ja|備中国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The name also appears in the form of '''villages, postal relay stations (eki), and shrines''' in various regions, including {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Izumi Province|ja|和泉国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
The '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部, also written 鳥甘部) appears exclusively in historical records from {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Based on place names, they were likely concentrated in the '''Karumura and Iware''' (磐余) regions, with additional settlements in {{ill|Settsu Province|ja|摂津国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑前国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where there were villages and districts named '''Torikabe''' (鳥養村, 鳥養郷).

After their defeat, he fled but resisted capture by the imperial forces.<ref>『日本書紀』{{ill|Emperor Sushun|ja|崇峻天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}即位前紀（用明天皇2nd year 7月条）</ref>

[[ja:鳥取造]] [[ja:鳥取部]] [[ja:鳥養部]] [[ja:%E9%B3%A5%E9%A4%8A%E9%83%A8]] [[sn:Torikai-be]] [[sn:orikai-be]]
<!--Torikai-be--> <!--Torikai-be--> [[Category:Ja import failed]]

'''Torikaibe''' (鳥養部) was a professional group (品部) in Japan before the [[Taika Reform]] that was responsible for raising and breeding birds captured by the [[Toribe]] (鳥取部) at the imperial court's request. It was also written as "Torigai-be" or "Toriame-be" (鳥飼部・鳥甘部). This article also covers the [[Toribe]] (ととりべ), another occupational group that specialized in capturing waterfowl for the court.
== Overview (概要) ==
The '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) oversaw the '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部), a specialized occupational group (*be*) responsible for capturing ornamental waterfowl. Meanwhile, the '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部) was likely responsible for the care and breeding of these captured birds.
In the central administration, high-Ranking {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were given the title of '''"Zō"''' (造), later changed to '''"Muraji"''' (連), while lower-Ranking officials held titles such as '''"Omi"''' (臣), '''"Atonae"''' (直), '''"Zō"''' (造), and '''"Kubi"''' (首).<ref>『日本書紀』(二)補注p349、岩波文庫、1994th year </ref>
The descendants of {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河桁命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were known as '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and '''Totori Muraji''' (鳥取連) in the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. His son, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, was also identified as the ancestor of the '''Totori Muraji''' in the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
The '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部) is documented in historical records from {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Bitchū Province|ja|備中国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The name also appears in the form of '''villages, postal relay stations (eki), and shrines''' in various regions, including {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Izumi Province|ja|和泉国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
The '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部, also written 鳥甘部) appears exclusively in historical records from {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Based on place names, they were likely concentrated in the '''Karumura and Iware''' (磐余) regions, with additional settlements in {{ill|Settsu Province|ja|摂津国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑前国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where there were villages and districts named '''Torikabe''' (鳥養村, 鳥養郷).
== Origins (由来) ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi|ja|誉津別命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (ほむちわけ の おおきみ) did not speak for a long time even after reaching adulthood, possibly due to the shock of his mother’s death, {{ill|Saho-hime|ja|狭穂姫命|lt=Saho-hime no Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. One day, while on a boat excursion, he heard the voice of a {{ill|Crested ibis|ja|鵠|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (くぐい) flying high in the sky and moved his jaw, uttering his first words.
His father, the emperor, dispatched {{ill|Yamanobe no Ōtaka|ja|山辺大鶙|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to chase the ibis across {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Harima Province|ja|播磨国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tajima Province|ja|但馬国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, until finally capturing it at Wanaumi no Minato (和那美之水門) in the {{ill|Koshi region|ja|越国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} using a trap net. However, the prince still could not speak.
Later, in a dream, the emperor was visited by {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who instructed him to build a shrine. It was decided that Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi should make a pilgrimage to {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with his attendant selected by divination. {{ill|Aketatsu no Ōkimi|ja|曙立王|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was chosen and, to prove the divine message, he performed a {{ill|ukei|ja|うけい|qq=draft|qq=draft}} by reviving a {{ill|heron|ja|鷺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and an {{ill|evergreen oak|ja|樫|qq=draft|qq=draft}} tree.
Emperor Suinin, convinced, sent Aketatsu no Ōkimi along with his younger brother, {{ill|Unakami no Ōkimi|ja|菟上王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, to accompany the prince to Izumo. There, the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} prepared a temporary shrine decorated with fresh green leaves and constructed an artificial hill. As they prepared to dine by the lower reaches of the {{ill|Hii River|ja|斐伊川|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the prince finally spoke, asking about the artificial hill. This event led to the establishment of the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Torikamebe|ja|鳥甘部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Homujibe|ja|品遅部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōyu-e|ja|大湯坐|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Wakayu-e|ja|若湯坐|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>『古事記』中巻、垂仁天皇条より</ref>
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Prince Homutsuwake|ja|誉津別皇子|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, son of Emperor Suinin, did not speak even after growing a beard and continued to cry like a child. One day, he saw a crested ibis and uttered his first words: "What is that?" The emperor then sent {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawatana|ja|天湯河板挙|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to chase the ibis to Izumo (or Tajima, according to another account), where he captured it. As a result, the prince was able to speak.
The emperor then granted Ame-no-Yukawatana the surname "Tottori no Miyatsuko" (鳥取造), meaning "head of the bird-catchers," and established the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Toriyaube|ja|鳥養部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Homutsube|ja|誉津部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>『日本書紀』垂仁天皇23rd year 10月8日条、11月2日条</ref>
{{ill|Hōga Toshio|ja|宝賀寿男|qq=draft|qq=draft}} suggests that, like the {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (horse breeders) and the {{ill|Ikaibe|ja|猪養部|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (boar hunters), members of the Toriyaube (鳥養部) likely bore facial tattoos (黥, mesaki) as part of their occupation. Although the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} mentions punitive tattoos, he argues that tattooing originally served a protective, magical purpose against animal attacks. He further suggests that the punitive interpretation arose later, when tattooing became associated with criminal punishment.<ref>「[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/hitori/irezumi.htm 入墨についての雑考]」『古樹紀之房間』、2010th year 。</ref>
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Musa no Suguri Ao|ja|身狭村主青|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and his men transported imported {{ill|geese|ja|ガチョウ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from the {{ill|Southern Dynasties (China)|ja|南朝|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, a dog belonging to either {{ill|Minuma no Kimi|ja|水沼県主|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or, in another account, {{ill|Mine no Agatanushi Numaro|ja|嶺県主泥麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, killed one of the birds. In a panic, the dog’s owner offered ten white swans and several "Toriyaubito" (養鳥人, birdkeepers) as compensation.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 9月4日条</ref>
The emperor settled these birdkeepers in Karumura and Iwaremura.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 10月7日条</ref>
Later, when a bird raised by the "Tori no Tsukasa" (鳥官, the imperial birdkeepers) was killed by a pet dog in {{ill|Uda District|ja|宇陀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the emperor punished the dog’s owner by tattooing his face and assigning him to the Toriyaube. The same punishment was imposed on men from {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} who were serving in forced labor and had spoken out against the emperor’s decision.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇11th year 10月条</ref>
{{ill|Tanigawa Ken'ichi|ja|谷川健一|qq=draft|qq=draft}} has noted a deep connection between metal refining and bird-related myths. He argues that the name "Homutsuwake" implies a birth in fire and that "Yue" (湯坐) secretly carries the meaning of "molten metal." He also points to links between {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawatana|ja|天湯河板挙|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, suggesting that the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who worshipped the thunder god {{ill|Nigi-hayahinomikoto|ja|ニギハヤヒ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, may have overseen the Toriyaube.<ref>『白鳥伝説』(上)p215 - p238、集英社文庫</ref>
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} also records the story of {{ill|Totori-be no Yorozu|ja|捕鳥部万|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a retainer of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who defended the clan’s estate during the {{ill|Battle against Mononobe no Moriya|ja|物部守屋|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the second year of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (587). After their defeat, he fled but resisted capture by the imperial forces.<ref>『日本書紀』{{ill|Emperor Sushun|ja|崇峻天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}即位前紀（用明天皇2nd year 7月条）</ref>

{{ill|Hōga Toshio|ja|宝賀寿男|qq=draft|qq=draft}} suggests that, like the {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (horse breeders) and the {{ill|Ikaibe|ja|猪養部|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (boar hunters), members of the Toriyaube (鳥養部) likely bore facial tattoos (黥, mesaki) as part of their occupation. Although the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} mentions punitive tattoos, he argues that tattooing originally served a protective, magical purpose against animal attacks. He further suggests that the punitive interpretation arose later, when tattooing became associated with criminal punishment.<ref>「[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/hitori/irezumi.htm 入墨についての雑考]」『古樹紀之房間』、2010th year 。</ref>
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Musa no Suguri Ao|ja|身狭村主青|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and his men transported imported {{ill|geese|ja|ガチョウ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from the {{ill|Southern Dynasties (China)|ja|南朝|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, a dog belonging to either {{ill|Minuma no Kimi|ja|水沼県主|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or, in another account, {{ill|Mine no Agatanushi Numaro|ja|嶺県主泥麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, killed one of the birds. In a panic, the dog’s owner offered ten white swans and several "Toriyaubito" (養鳥人, birdkeepers) as compensation.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 9月4日条</ref>
The emperor settled these birdkeepers in Karumura and Iwaremura.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 10月7日条</ref>
Later, when a bird raised by the "Tori no Tsukasa" (鳥官, the imperial birdkeepers) was killed by a pet dog in {{ill|Uda District|ja|宇陀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the emperor punished the dog’s owner by tattooing his face and assigning him to the Toriyaube. The same punishment was imposed on men from {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} who were serving in forced labor and had spoken out against the emperor’s decision.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇11th year 10月条</ref>
{{ill|Tanigawa Ken'ichi|ja|谷川健一|qq=draft|qq=draft}} has noted a deep connection between metal refining and bird-related myths. He argues that the name "Homutsuwake" implies a birth in fire and that "Yue" (湯坐) secretly carries the meaning of "molten metal." He also points to links between {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawatana|ja|天湯河板挙|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, suggesting that the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who worshipped the thunder god {{ill|Nigi-hayahinomikoto|ja|ニギハヤヒ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, may have overseen the Toriyaube.<ref>『白鳥伝説』(上)p215 - p238、集英社文庫</ref>
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} also records the story of {{ill|Totori-be no Yorozu|ja|捕鳥部万|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a retainer of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who defended the clan’s estate during the {{ill|Battle against Mononobe no Moriya|ja|物部守屋|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the second year of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (587). After their defeat, he fled but resisted capture by the imperial forces.<ref>『日本書紀』{{ill|Emperor Sushun|ja|崇峻天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}即位前紀（用明天皇2nd year 7月条）</ref>
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Kadokawa Nihonshi Jiten'' (2nd Edition), p. 699, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, 1974, Kadokawa Shoten * ''Kojiki'' (Complete Annotated Japanese Classics, Vol. 1), Shogakukan, 1983 * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volumes 2-4), Iwanami Bunko, 1994-1995 * ''The Legend of the White Bird'' (Volume 1), Kenichi Tanigawa, Shueisha Bunko, 1988 == See Also == *{{ill|Be System|ja|部民制|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - {{ill|Inukaibe|ja|犬養部|qq=draft|qq=draft}} / {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|qq=draft|qq=draft}} *{{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - {{ill|Tottori City|ja|鳥取市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} *{{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} *{{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} *{{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} *{{ill|Battle of Shigisan|ja|丁未の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

revision.text:
'''Torikaibe''' (鳥養部) was a professional group (品部) in Japan before the [[Taika Reform]] that was responsible for raising and breeding birds captured by the [[Toribe]] (鳥取部) at the imperial court's request. It was also written as "Torigai-be" or "Toriame-be" (鳥飼部・鳥甘部). This article also covers the [[Toribe]] (ととりべ), another occupational group that specialized in capturing waterfowl for the court.
== Overview (概要) ==
The '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) oversaw the '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部), a specialized occupational group (*be*) responsible for capturing ornamental waterfowl. Meanwhile, the '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部) was likely responsible for the care and breeding of these captured birds.
In the central administration, high-Ranking {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were given the title of '''"Zō"''' (造), later changed to '''"Muraji"''' (連), while lower-Ranking officials held titles such as '''"Omi"''' (臣), '''"Atonae"''' (直), '''"Zō"''' (造), and '''"Kubi"''' (首).<ref>『日本書紀』(二)補注p349、岩波文庫、1994th year </ref>
The descendants of {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河桁命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were known as '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and '''Totori Muraji''' (鳥取連) in the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. His son, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, was also identified as the ancestor of the '''Totori Muraji''' in the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
The '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部) is documented in historical records from {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Bitchū Province|ja|備中国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The name also appears in the form of '''villages, postal relay stations (eki), and shrines''' in various regions, including {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Izumi Province|ja|和泉国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
The '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部, also written 鳥甘部) appears exclusively in historical records from {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Based on place names, they were likely concentrated in the '''Karumura and Iware''' (磐余) regions, with additional settlements in {{ill|Settsu Province|ja|摂津国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑前国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where there were villages and districts named '''Torikabe''' (鳥養村, 鳥養郷).
== Origins (由来) ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi|ja|誉津別命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (ほむちわけ の おおきみ) did not speak for a long time even after reaching adulthood, possibly due to the shock of his mother’s death, {{ill|Saho-hime|ja|狭穂姫命|lt=Saho-hime no Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. One day, while on a boat excursion, he heard the voice of a {{ill|Crested ibis|ja|鵠|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (くぐい) flying high in the sky and moved his jaw, uttering his first words.
His father, the emperor, dispatched {{ill|Yamanobe no Ōtaka|ja|山辺大鶙|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to chase the ibis across {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Harima Province|ja|播磨国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tajima Province|ja|但馬国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, until finally capturing it at Wanaumi no Minato (和那美之水門) in the {{ill|Koshi region|ja|越国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} using a trap net. However, the prince still could not speak.
Later, in a dream, the emperor was visited by {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who instructed him to build a shrine. It was decided that Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi should make a pilgrimage to {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with his attendant selected by divination. {{ill|Aketatsu no Ōkimi|ja|曙立王|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was chosen and, to prove the divine message, he performed a {{ill|ukei|ja|うけい|qq=draft|qq=draft}} by reviving a {{ill|heron|ja|鷺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and an {{ill|evergreen oak|ja|樫|qq=draft|qq=draft}} tree.
Emperor Suinin, convinced, sent Aketatsu no Ōkimi along with his younger brother, {{ill|Unakami no Ōkimi|ja|菟上王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, to accompany the prince to Izumo. There, the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} prepared a temporary shrine decorated with fresh green leaves and constructed an artificial hill. As they prepared to dine by the lower reaches of the {{ill|Hii River|ja|斐伊川|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the prince finally spoke, asking about the artificial hill. This event led to the establishment of the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Torikamebe|ja|鳥甘部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Homujibe|ja|品遅部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōyu-e|ja|大湯坐|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Wakayu-e|ja|若湯坐|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>『古事記』中巻、垂仁天皇条より</ref>
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Prince Homutsuwake|ja|誉津別皇子|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, son of Emperor Suinin, did not speak even after growing a beard and continued to cry like a child. One day, he saw a crested ibis and uttered his first words: "What is that?" The emperor then sent {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawatana|ja|天湯河板挙|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to chase the ibis to Izumo (or Tajima, according to another account), where he captured it. As a result, the prince was able to speak.
The emperor then granted Ame-no-Yukawatana the surname "Tottori no Miyatsuko" (鳥取造), meaning "head of the bird-catchers," and established the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Toriyaube|ja|鳥養部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Homutsube|ja|誉津部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>『日本書紀』垂仁天皇23rd year 10月8日条、11月2日条</ref>
{{ill|Hōga Toshio|ja|宝賀寿男|qq=draft|qq=draft}} suggests that, like the {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (horse breeders) and the {{ill|Ikaibe|ja|猪養部|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (boar hunters), members of the Toriyaube (鳥養部) likely bore facial tattoos (黥, mesaki) as part of their occupation. Although the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} mentions punitive tattoos, he argues that tattooing originally served a protective, magical purpose against animal attacks. He further suggests that the punitive interpretation arose later, when tattooing became associated with criminal punishment.<ref>「[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/hitori/irezumi.htm 入墨についての雑考]」『古樹紀之房間』、2010th year 。</ref>
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Musa no Suguri Ao|ja|身狭村主青|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and his men transported imported {{ill|geese|ja|ガチョウ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from the {{ill|Southern Dynasties (China)|ja|南朝|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, a dog belonging to either {{ill|Minuma no Kimi|ja|水沼県主|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or, in another account, {{ill|Mine no Agatanushi Numaro|ja|嶺県主泥麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, killed one of the birds. In a panic, the dog’s owner offered ten white swans and several "Toriyaubito" (養鳥人, birdkeepers) as compensation.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 9月4日条</ref>
The emperor settled these birdkeepers in Karumura and Iwaremura.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 10月7日条</ref>
Later, when a bird raised by the "Tori no Tsukasa" (鳥官, the imperial birdkeepers) was killed by a pet dog in {{ill|Uda District|ja|宇陀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the emperor punished the dog’s owner by tattooing his face and assigning him to the Toriyaube. The same punishment was imposed on men from {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} who were serving in forced labor and had spoken out against the emperor’s decision.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇11th year 10月条</ref>
{{ill|Tanigawa Ken'ichi|ja|谷川健一|qq=draft|qq=draft}} has noted a deep connection between metal refining and bird-related myths. He argues that the name "Homutsuwake" implies a birth in fire and that "Yue" (湯坐) secretly carries the meaning of "molten metal." He also points to links between {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawatana|ja|天湯河板挙|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, suggesting that the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who worshipped the thunder god {{ill|Nigi-hayahinomikoto|ja|ニギハヤヒ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, may have overseen the Toriyaube.<ref>『白鳥伝説』(上)p215 - p238、集英社文庫</ref>
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} also records the story of {{ill|Totori-be no Yorozu|ja|捕鳥部万|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a retainer of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who defended the clan’s estate during the {{ill|Battle against Mononobe no Moriya|ja|物部守屋|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the second year of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (587). After their defeat, he fled but resisted capture by the imperial forces.<ref>『日本書紀』{{ill|Emperor Sushun|ja|崇峻天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}即位前紀（用明天皇2nd year 7月条）</ref>
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Kadokawa Nihonshi Jiten'' (2nd Edition), p. 699, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, 1974, Kadokawa Shoten * ''Kojiki'' (Complete Annotated Japanese Classics, Vol. 1), Shogakukan, 1983 * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volumes 2-4), Iwanami Bunko, 1994-1995 * ''The Legend of the White Bird'' (Volume 1), Kenichi Tanigawa, Shueisha Bunko, 1988 == See Also == *{{ill|Be System|ja|部民制|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - {{ill|Inukaibe|ja|犬養部|qq=draft|qq=draft}} / {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|qq=draft|qq=draft}} *{{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - {{ill|Tottori City|ja|鳥取市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} *{{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} *{{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} *{{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} *{{ill|Battle of Shigisan|ja|丁未の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi|ja|誉津別命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (ほむちわけ の おおきみ) did not speak for a long time even after reaching adulthood, possibly due to the shock of his mother’s death, {{ill|Saho-hime|ja|狭穂姫命|lt=Saho-hime no Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. One day, while on a boat excursion, he heard the voice of a {{ill|Crested ibis|ja|鵠|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (くぐい) flying high in the sky and moved his jaw, uttering his first words.
His father, the emperor, dispatched {{ill|Yamanobe no Ōtaka|ja|山辺大鶙|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to chase the ibis across {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Harima Province|ja|播磨国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tajima Province|ja|但馬国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, until finally capturing it at Wanaumi no Minato (和那美之水門) in the {{ill|Koshi region|ja|越国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} using a trap net. However, the prince still could not speak.
Later, in a dream, the emperor was visited by {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who instructed him to build a shrine. It was decided that Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi should make a pilgrimage to {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with his attendant selected by divination. {{ill|Aketatsu no Ōkimi|ja|曙立王|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was chosen and, to prove the divine message, he performed a {{ill|ukei|ja|うけい|qq=draft|qq=draft}} by reviving a {{ill|heron|ja|鷺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and an {{ill|evergreen oak|ja|樫|qq=draft|qq=draft}} tree.
Emperor Suinin, convinced, sent Aketatsu no Ōkimi along with his younger brother, {{ill|Unakami no Ōkimi|ja|菟上王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, to accompany the prince to Izumo. There, the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} prepared a temporary shrine decorated with fresh green leaves and constructed an artificial hill. As they prepared to dine by the lower reaches of the {{ill|Hii River|ja|斐伊川|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the prince finally spoke, asking about the artificial hill. This event led to the establishment of the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Torikamebe|ja|鳥甘部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Homujibe|ja|品遅部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōyu-e|ja|大湯坐|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Wakayu-e|ja|若湯坐|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>『古事記』中巻、垂仁天皇条より</ref>
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Prince Homutsuwake|ja|誉津別皇子|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, son of Emperor Suinin, did not speak even after growing a beard and continued to cry like a child. One day, he saw a crested ibis and uttered his first words: "What is that?" The emperor then sent {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawatana|ja|天湯河板挙|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to chase the ibis to Izumo (or Tajima, according to another account), where he captured it. As a result, the prince was able to speak.
The emperor then granted Ame-no-Yukawatana the surname "Tottori no Miyatsuko" (鳥取造), meaning "head of the bird-catchers," and established the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Toriyaube|ja|鳥養部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Homutsube|ja|誉津部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>『日本書紀』垂仁天皇23rd year 10月8日条、11月2日条</ref>
{{ill|Hōga Toshio|ja|宝賀寿男|qq=draft|qq=draft}} suggests that, like the {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (horse breeders) and the {{ill|Ikaibe|ja|猪養部|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (boar hunters), members of the Toriyaube (鳥養部) likely bore facial tattoos (黥, mesaki) as part of their occupation. Although the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} mentions punitive tattoos, he argues that tattooing originally served a protective, magical purpose against animal attacks. He further suggests that the punitive interpretation arose later, when tattooing became associated with criminal punishment.<ref>「[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/hitori/irezumi.htm 入墨についての雑考]」『古樹紀之房間』、2010th year 。</ref>
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Musa no Suguri Ao|ja|身狭村主青|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and his men transported imported {{ill|geese|ja|ガチョウ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from the {{ill|Southern Dynasties (China)|ja|南朝|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, a dog belonging to either {{ill|Minuma no Kimi|ja|水沼県主|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or, in another account, {{ill|Mine no Agatanushi Numaro|ja|嶺県主泥麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, killed one of the birds. In a panic, the dog’s owner offered ten white swans and several "Toriyaubito" (養鳥人, birdkeepers) as compensation.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 9月4日条</ref>
The emperor settled these birdkeepers in Karumura and Iwaremura.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 10月7日条</ref>
Later, when a bird raised by the "Tori no Tsukasa" (鳥官, the imperial birdkeepers) was killed by a pet dog in {{ill|Uda District|ja|宇陀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the emperor punished the dog’s owner by tattooing his face and assigning him to the Toriyaube. The same punishment was imposed on men from {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} who were serving in forced labor and had spoken out against the emperor’s decision.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇11th year 10月条</ref>
{{ill|Tanigawa Ken'ichi|ja|谷川健一|qq=draft|qq=draft}} has noted a deep connection between metal refining and bird-related myths. He argues that the name "Homutsuwake" implies a birth in fire and that "Yue" (湯坐) secretly carries the meaning of "molten metal." He also points to links between {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawatana|ja|天湯河板挙|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, suggesting that the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who worshipped the thunder god {{ill|Nigi-hayahinomikoto|ja|ニギハヤヒ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, may have overseen the Toriyaube.<ref>『白鳥伝説』(上)p215 - p238、集英社文庫</ref>
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} also records the story of {{ill|Totori-be no Yorozu|ja|捕鳥部万|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a retainer of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who defended the clan’s estate during the {{ill|Battle against Mononobe no Moriya|ja|物部守屋|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the second year of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (587). After their defeat, he fled but resisted capture by the imperial forces.<ref>『日本書紀』{{ill|Emperor Sushun|ja|崇峻天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}即位前紀（用明天皇2nd year 7月条）</ref>
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Kadokawa Nihonshi Jiten'' (2nd Edition), p. 699, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, 1974, Kadokawa Shoten * ''Kojiki'' (Complete Annotated Japanese Classics, Vol. 1), Shogakukan, 1983 * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volumes 2-4), Iwanami Bunko, 1994-1995 * ''The Legend of the White Bird'' (Volume 1), Kenichi Tanigawa, Shueisha Bunko, 1988 == See Also == *{{ill|Be System|ja|部民制|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - {{ill|Inukaibe|ja|犬養部|qq=draft|qq=draft}} / {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|qq=draft|qq=draft}} *{{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - {{ill|Tottori City|ja|鳥取市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} *{{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} *{{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} *{{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} *{{ill|Battle of Shigisan|ja|丁未の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

revision.text:
'''Torikaibe''' (鳥養部) was a professional group (品部) in Japan before the [[Taika Reform]] that was responsible for raising and breeding birds captured by the [[Toribe]] (鳥取部) at the imperial court's request. It was also written as "Torigai-be" or "Toriame-be" (鳥飼部・鳥甘部). This article also covers the [[Toribe]] (ととりべ), another occupational group that specialized in capturing waterfowl for the court.
== Overview (概要) ==
The '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) oversaw the '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部), a specialized occupational group (*be*) responsible for capturing ornamental waterfowl. Meanwhile, the '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部) was likely responsible for the care and breeding of these captured birds.
In the central administration, high-Ranking {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were given the title of '''"Zō"''' (造), later changed to '''"Muraji"''' (連), while lower-Ranking officials held titles such as '''"Omi"''' (臣), '''"Atonae"''' (直), '''"Zō"''' (造), and '''"Kubi"''' (首).<ref>『日本書紀』(二)補注p349、岩波文庫、1994th year </ref>
The descendants of {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河桁命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were known as '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and '''Totori Muraji''' (鳥取連) in the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. His son, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, was also identified as the ancestor of the '''Totori Muraji''' in the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
The '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部) is documented in historical records from {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Bitchū Province|ja|備中国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The name also appears in the form of '''villages, postal relay stations (eki), and shrines''' in various regions, including {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Izumi Province|ja|和泉国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Higo Province|ja|肥後国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
The '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部, also written 鳥甘部) appears exclusively in historical records from {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Based on place names, they were likely concentrated in the '''Karumura and Iware''' (磐余) regions, with additional settlements in {{ill|Settsu Province|ja|摂津国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Chikuzen Province|ja|筑前国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Chikugo Province|ja|筑後国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where there were villages and districts named '''Torikabe''' (鳥養村, 鳥養郷).
== Origins (由来) ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi|ja|誉津別命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (ほむちわけ の おおきみ) did not speak for a long time even after reaching adulthood, possibly due to the shock of his mother’s death, {{ill|Saho-hime|ja|狭穂姫命|lt=Saho-hime no Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. One day, while on a boat excursion, he heard the voice of a {{ill|Crested ibis|ja|鵠|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (くぐい) flying high in the sky and moved his jaw, uttering his first words.
His father, the emperor, dispatched {{ill|Yamanobe no Ōtaka|ja|山辺大鶙|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to chase the ibis across {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Harima Province|ja|播磨国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tajima Province|ja|但馬国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, until finally capturing it at Wanaumi no Minato (和那美之水門) in the {{ill|Koshi region|ja|越国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} using a trap net. However, the prince still could not speak.
Later, in a dream, the emperor was visited by {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who instructed him to build a shrine. It was decided that Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi should make a pilgrimage to {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with his attendant selected by divination. {{ill|Aketatsu no Ōkimi|ja|曙立王|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was chosen and, to prove the divine message, he performed a {{ill|ukei|ja|うけい|qq=draft|qq=draft}} by reviving a {{ill|heron|ja|鷺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and an {{ill|evergreen oak|ja|樫|qq=draft|qq=draft}} tree.
Emperor Suinin, convinced, sent Aketatsu no Ōkimi along with his younger brother, {{ill|Unakami no Ōkimi|ja|菟上王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, to accompany the prince to Izumo. There, the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} prepared a temporary shrine decorated with fresh green leaves and constructed an artificial hill. As they prepared to dine by the lower reaches of the {{ill|Hii River|ja|斐伊川|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the prince finally spoke, asking about the artificial hill. This event led to the establishment of the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Torikamebe|ja|鳥甘部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Homujibe|ja|品遅部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōyu-e|ja|大湯坐|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Wakayu-e|ja|若湯坐|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>『古事記』中巻、垂仁天皇条より</ref>
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Prince Homutsuwake|ja|誉津別皇子|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, son of Emperor Suinin, did not speak even after growing a beard and continued to cry like a child. One day, he saw a crested ibis and uttered his first words: "What is that?" The emperor then sent {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawatana|ja|天湯河板挙|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to chase the ibis to Izumo (or Tajima, according to another account), where he captured it. As a result, the prince was able to speak.
The emperor then granted Ame-no-Yukawatana the surname "Tottori no Miyatsuko" (鳥取造), meaning "head of the bird-catchers," and established the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Toriyaube|ja|鳥養部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Homutsube|ja|誉津部|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>『日本書紀』垂仁天皇23rd year 10月8日条、11月2日条</ref>
{{ill|Hōga Toshio|ja|宝賀寿男|qq=draft|qq=draft}} suggests that, like the {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (horse breeders) and the {{ill|Ikaibe|ja|猪養部|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (boar hunters), members of the Toriyaube (鳥養部) likely bore facial tattoos (黥, mesaki) as part of their occupation. Although the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} mentions punitive tattoos, he argues that tattooing originally served a protective, magical purpose against animal attacks. He further suggests that the punitive interpretation arose later, when tattooing became associated with criminal punishment.<ref>「[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/hitori/irezumi.htm 入墨についての雑考]」『古樹紀之房間』、2010th year 。</ref>
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Musa no Suguri Ao|ja|身狭村主青|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and his men transported imported {{ill|geese|ja|ガチョウ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from the {{ill|Southern Dynasties (China)|ja|南朝|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, a dog belonging to either {{ill|Minuma no Kimi|ja|水沼県主|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or, in another account, {{ill|Mine no Agatanushi Numaro|ja|嶺県主泥麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, killed one of the birds. In a panic, the dog’s owner offered ten white swans and several "Toriyaubito" (養鳥人, birdkeepers) as compensation.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 9月4日条</ref>
The emperor settled these birdkeepers in Karumura and Iwaremura.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 10月7日条</ref>
Later, when a bird raised by the "Tori no Tsukasa" (鳥官, the imperial birdkeepers) was killed by a pet dog in {{ill|Uda District|ja|宇陀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the emperor punished the dog’s owner by tattooing his face and assigning him to the Toriyaube. The same punishment was imposed on men from {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} who were serving in forced labor and had spoken out against the emperor’s decision.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇11th year 10月条</ref>

He argues that the name "Homutsuwake" implies a birth in fire and that "Yue" (湯坐) secretly carries the meaning of "molten metal." After their defeat, he fled but resisted capture by the imperial forces.<ref>『日本書紀』{{ill|Emperor Sushun|ja|崇峻天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}即位前紀（用明天皇2nd year 7月条）</ref>

== References == * ''Kadokawa Nihonshi Jiten'' (2nd Edition), p. 699, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, 1974, Kadokawa Shoten * ''Kojiki'' (Complete Annotated Japanese Classics, Vol. 1), Shogakukan, 1983 * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volumes 2-4), Iwanami Bunko, 1994-1995 * ''The Legend of the White Bird'' (Volume 1), Kenichi Tanigawa, Shueisha Bunko, 1988 == See Also == *{{ill|Be System|ja|部民制|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - {{ill|Inukaibe|ja|犬養部|qq=draft|qq=draft}} / {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|qq=draft|qq=draft}} *{{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - {{ill|Tottori City|ja|鳥取市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} *{{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} *{{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} *{{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} *{{ill|Battle of Shigisan|ja|丁未の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
