revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鳥養部]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鳥養部]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鳥養部]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q55525380]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Torikaibe''' (鳥養部) was a professional group (品部) in Japan before the [[Taika Reform]] that was responsible for raising and breeding birds captured by the [[Toribe]] (鳥取部) at the imperial court's request. It was also written as "Torigai-be" or "Toriame-be" (鳥飼部・鳥甘部). This article also covers the [[Toribe]] (ととりべ), another occupational group that specialized in capturing waterfowl for the court.
== Overview (概要) ==
The '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) oversaw the '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部), a specialized occupational group (*be*) responsible for capturing ornamental waterfowl. Meanwhile, the '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部) was likely responsible for the care and breeding of these captured birds.
In the central administration, high-Ranking {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomonomiyatsuko|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} were given the title of '''"Zō"''' (造), later changed to '''"Muraji"''' (連), while lower-Ranking officials held titles such as '''"Omi"''' (臣), '''"Atonae"''' (直), '''"Zō"''' (造), and '''"Kubi"''' (首).<ref>『日本書紀』(二)補注p349、岩波文庫、1994th year </ref>
The descendants of {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河桁命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawata|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天湯河板挙}} were known as '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] and '''Totori Muraji''' (鳥取連) in the [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]. His son, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, was also identified as the ancestor of the '''Totori Muraji''' in the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]].
The '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部) is documented in historical records from [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]], [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]], {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and [[:en:Bitchū Province|Bitchū Province]]. The name also appears in the form of '''villages, postal relay stations (eki), and shrines''' in various regions, including [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]], [[:en:Izumi Province|Izumi Province]], {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Etchū Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]], {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Bizen Province|Bizen Province]], [[:en:Higo Province|Higo Province]], [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]], and [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]].
The '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部, also written 鳥甘部) appears exclusively in historical records from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]. Based on place names, they were likely concentrated in the '''Karumura and Iware''' (磐余) regions, with additional settlements in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], [[:en:Chikuzen Province|Chikuzen Province]], and [[:en:Chikugo Province|Chikugo Province]], where there were villages and districts named '''Torikabe''' (鳥養村, 鳥養郷).
== Origins (由来) ==
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], {{ill|Homutsuwake no Mikoto|lt=Homutsuwake no Mikoto|lt=Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi|ja|誉津別命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi|qq=}} (ほむちわけ の おおきみ) did not speak for a long time even after reaching adulthood, possibly due to the shock of his mother’s death, [[:en:Saho-hime|Saho-hime no Mikoto]]. One day, while on a boat excursion, he heard the voice of a {{ill|Crested ibis|ja|鵠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crested ibis|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハクチョウ|1=swan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q34384}} (くぐい) flying high in the sky and moved his jaw, uttering his first words.
His father, the emperor, dispatched {{ill|Yamanobe no Ōtaka|ja|山辺大鶙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Ōtaka|1=Yamabe no Ōtaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52319925}} to chase the ibis across [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]], [[:en:Harima Province|Harima Province]], {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], [[:en:Tajima Province|Tajima Province]], [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]], [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]], [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]], and [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], until finally capturing it at Wanaumi no Minato (和那美之水門) in the [[:en:Koshi Province (Japan)|Koshi region]] using a trap net. However, the prince still could not speak.
Later, in a dream, the emperor was visited by {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, who instructed him to build a shrine. It was decided that Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi should make a pilgrimage to [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], with his attendant selected by divination. {{ill|Aketatsu no Ōkimi|ja|曙立王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aketatsu no Ōkimi|1=Aketatsu no Ōkimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11515056}} was chosen and, to prove the divine message, he performed a [[:en:Ukehi|ukei]] by reviving a {{ill|heron|ja|鷺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/heron|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サギ|1=heron|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3259077}} and an {{ill|evergreen oak|ja|樫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/evergreen oak|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カシ|1=Cyclobalanopsis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q796117}} tree.
Emperor Suinin, convinced, sent Aketatsu no Ōkimi along with his younger brother, {{ill|Unakami no Ōkimi|ja|菟上王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unakami no Ōkimi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, to accompany the prince to Izumo. There, the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} prepared a temporary shrine decorated with fresh green leaves and constructed an artificial hill. As they prepared to dine by the lower reaches of the [[:en:Hii River|Hii River]], the prince finally spoke, asking about the artificial hill. This event led to the establishment of the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottoribe|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鳥養部|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, {{ill|Torikamebe|ja|鳥甘部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Torikamebe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Homujibe|ja|品遅部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homujibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ōyu-e|ja|大湯坐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyu-e|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Wakayu-e|ja|若湯坐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayu-e|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『古事記』中巻、垂仁天皇条より</ref>
According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], {{ill|Prince Homutsuwake|ja|誉津別皇子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Homutsuwake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, son of Emperor Suinin, did not speak even after growing a beard and continued to cry like a child. One day, he saw a crested ibis and uttered his first words: "What is that?" The emperor then sent {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河板挙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawatana|qq=}} to chase the ibis to Izumo (or Tajima, according to another account), where he captured it. As a result, the prince was able to speak.
The emperor then granted Ame-no-Yukawatana the surname "Tottori no Miyatsuko" (鳥取造), meaning "head of the bird-catchers," and established the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottoribe|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鳥養部|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, {{ill|Toriyaube|ja|鳥養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toriyaube|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, and {{ill|Homutsube|ja|誉津部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homutsube|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『日本書紀』垂仁天皇23rd year 10月8日条、11月2日条</ref>
{{ill|Toshio Hōga|lt=Toshio Hōga|lt=Hōga Toshio|ja|宝賀寿男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōga Toshio|qq=}} suggests that, like the {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umakaibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (horse breeders) and the {{ill|Ikaibe|ja|猪養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikaibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (boar hunters), members of the Toriyaube (鳥養部) likely bore facial tattoos (黥, mesaki) as part of their occupation. Although the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] mentions punitive tattoos, he argues that tattooing originally served a protective, magical purpose against animal attacks. He further suggests that the punitive interpretation arose later, when tattooing became associated with criminal punishment.<ref>「[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/hitori/irezumi.htm 入墨についての雑考]」『古樹紀之房間』、2010th year 。</ref>
During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]], {{ill|Musa no Suguri Ao|ja|身狭村主青|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musa no Suguri Ao|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 身狭青|1=Musa no Suguri Ao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52320012}} and his men transported imported [[:en:Domestic goose|geese]] from the {{ill|Southern Dynasties (China)|ja|南朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Dynasties (China)|1=Southern Dynasties|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1206988}} to [[:en:Tsukushi Province|Tsukushi Province]]. However, a dog belonging to either {{ill|Minuma no Kimi|ja|水沼県主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minuma no Kimi|1=Minuma no Kimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548885}} or, in another account, {{ill|Mine no Agatanushi Numaro|ja|嶺県主泥麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mine no Agatanushi Numaro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, killed one of the birds. In a panic, the dog’s owner offered ten white swans and several "Toriyaubito" (養鳥人, birdkeepers) as compensation.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 9月4日条</ref>
The emperor settled these birdkeepers in Karumura and Iwaremura.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 10月7日条</ref>
Later, when a bird raised by the "Tori no Tsukasa" (鳥官, the imperial birdkeepers) was killed by a pet dog in {{ill|Uda District|ja|宇陀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uda District|1=Uda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11449912}}, the emperor punished the dog’s owner by tattooing his face and assigning him to the Toriyaube. The same punishment was imposed on men from [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]] and [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] who were serving in forced labor and had spoken out against the emperor’s decision.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇11th year 10月条</ref>
{{ill|Ken'ichi Tanigawa|lt=Ken'ichi Tanigawa|lt=Tanigawa Ken'ichi|ja|谷川健一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanigawa Ken'ichi|qq=}} has noted a deep connection between metal refining and bird-related myths. He argues that the name "Homutsuwake" implies a birth in fire and that "Yue" (湯坐) secretly carries the meaning of "molten metal." He also points to links between {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河板挙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawatana|qq=}} and {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, suggesting that the [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]], who worshipped the thunder god {{ill|Nigi-hayahinomikoto|ja|ニギハヤヒ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigi-hayahinomikoto|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}, may have overseen the Toriyaube.<ref>『白鳥伝説』(上)p215 - p238、集英社文庫</ref>
The [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] also records the story of [[:en:Totoribe no Yorozu|Totori-be no Yorozu]], a retainer of the [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]], who defended the clan’s estate during the [[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Battle against Mononobe no Moriya]] in the second year of [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]] (587). After their defeat, he fled but resisted capture by the imperial forces.<ref>『日本書紀』[[:en:Emperor Sushun|Emperor Sushun]]即位前紀（用明天皇2nd year 7月条）</ref>
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Kadokawa Nihonshi Jiten'' (2nd Edition), p. 699, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, 1974, Kadokawa Shoten * ''Kojiki'' (Complete Annotated Japanese Classics, Vol. 1), Shogakukan, 1983 * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volumes 2-4), Iwanami Bunko, 1994-1995 * ''The Legend of the White Bird'' (Volume 1), Kenichi Tanigawa, Shueisha Bunko, 1988 == See Also == *[[:en:Bemin|Be System]] - {{ill|Inukaibe|ja|犬養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inukaibe|qq=}} / {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umakaibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} *[[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]] - [[:en:Tottori (city)|Tottori City]] *{{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Shrine|1=Totori no kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48760952}} *[[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]] *[[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]] *[[:en:Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鳥養部]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鳥養部]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鳥養部]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q55525380]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Torikaibe''' (鳥養部) was a professional group (品部) in Japan before the [[Taika Reform]] that was responsible for raising and breeding birds captured by the [[Toribe]] (鳥取部) at the imperial court's request. It was also written as "Torigai-be" or "Toriame-be" (鳥飼部・鳥甘部). This article also covers the [[Toribe]] (ととりべ), another occupational group that specialized in capturing waterfowl for the court.
== Overview (概要) ==
The '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) oversaw the '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部), a specialized occupational group (*be*) responsible for capturing ornamental waterfowl. Meanwhile, the '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部) was likely responsible for the care and breeding of these captured birds.
In the central administration, high-Ranking {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomonomiyatsuko|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} were given the title of '''"Zō"''' (造), later changed to '''"Muraji"''' (連), while lower-Ranking officials held titles such as '''"Omi"''' (臣), '''"Atonae"''' (直), '''"Zō"''' (造), and '''"Kubi"''' (首).<ref>『日本書紀』(二)補注p349、岩波文庫、1994th year </ref>
The descendants of {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河桁命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawata|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天湯河板挙}} were known as '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] and '''Totori Muraji''' (鳥取連) in the [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]. His son, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, was also identified as the ancestor of the '''Totori Muraji''' in the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]].
The '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部) is documented in historical records from [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]], [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]], {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and [[:en:Bitchū Province|Bitchū Province]]. The name also appears in the form of '''villages, postal relay stations (eki), and shrines''' in various regions, including [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]], [[:en:Izumi Province|Izumi Province]], {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Etchū Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]], {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Bizen Province|Bizen Province]], [[:en:Higo Province|Higo Province]], [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]], and [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]].
The '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部, also written 鳥甘部) appears exclusively in historical records from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]. Based on place names, they were likely concentrated in the '''Karumura and Iware''' (磐余) regions, with additional settlements in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], [[:en:Chikuzen Province|Chikuzen Province]], and [[:en:Chikugo Province|Chikugo Province]], where there were villages and districts named '''Torikabe''' (鳥養村, 鳥養郷).
== Origins (由来) ==
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], {{ill|Homutsuwake no Mikoto|lt=Homutsuwake no Mikoto|lt=Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi|ja|誉津別命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi|qq=}} (ほむちわけ の おおきみ) did not speak for a long time even after reaching adulthood, possibly due to the shock of his mother’s death, [[:en:Saho-hime|Saho-hime no Mikoto]]. One day, while on a boat excursion, he heard the voice of a {{ill|Crested ibis|ja|鵠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crested ibis|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハクチョウ|1=swan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q34384}} (くぐい) flying high in the sky and moved his jaw, uttering his first words.
His father, the emperor, dispatched {{ill|Yamanobe no Ōtaka|ja|山辺大鶙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Ōtaka|1=Yamabe no Ōtaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52319925}} to chase the ibis across [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]], [[:en:Harima Province|Harima Province]], {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], [[:en:Tajima Province|Tajima Province]], [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]], [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]], [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]], and [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], until finally capturing it at Wanaumi no Minato (和那美之水門) in the [[:en:Koshi Province (Japan)|Koshi region]] using a trap net. However, the prince still could not speak.
Later, in a dream, the emperor was visited by {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, who instructed him to build a shrine. It was decided that Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi should make a pilgrimage to [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], with his attendant selected by divination. {{ill|Aketatsu no Ōkimi|ja|曙立王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aketatsu no Ōkimi|1=Aketatsu no Ōkimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11515056}} was chosen and, to prove the divine message, he performed a [[:en:Ukehi|ukei]] by reviving a {{ill|heron|ja|鷺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/heron|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サギ|1=heron|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3259077}} and an {{ill|evergreen oak|ja|樫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/evergreen oak|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カシ|1=Cyclobalanopsis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q796117}} tree.
Emperor Suinin, convinced, sent Aketatsu no Ōkimi along with his younger brother, {{ill|Unakami no Ōkimi|ja|菟上王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unakami no Ōkimi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, to accompany the prince to Izumo. There, the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} prepared a temporary shrine decorated with fresh green leaves and constructed an artificial hill. As they prepared to dine by the lower reaches of the [[:en:Hii River|Hii River]], the prince finally spoke, asking about the artificial hill. This event led to the establishment of the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottoribe|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鳥養部|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, {{ill|Torikamebe|ja|鳥甘部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Torikamebe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Homujibe|ja|品遅部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homujibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ōyu-e|ja|大湯坐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyu-e|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Wakayu-e|ja|若湯坐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayu-e|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『古事記』中巻、垂仁天皇条より</ref>
According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], {{ill|Prince Homutsuwake|ja|誉津別皇子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Homutsuwake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, son of Emperor Suinin, did not speak even after growing a beard and continued to cry like a child. One day, he saw a crested ibis and uttered his first words: "What is that?" The emperor then sent {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河板挙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawatana|qq=}} to chase the ibis to Izumo (or Tajima, according to another account), where he captured it. As a result, the prince was able to speak.
The emperor then granted Ame-no-Yukawatana the surname "Tottori no Miyatsuko" (鳥取造), meaning "head of the bird-catchers," and established the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottoribe|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鳥養部|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, {{ill|Toriyaube|ja|鳥養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toriyaube|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, and {{ill|Homutsube|ja|誉津部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homutsube|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『日本書紀』垂仁天皇23rd year 10月8日条、11月2日条</ref>
{{ill|Toshio Hōga|lt=Toshio Hōga|lt=Hōga Toshio|ja|宝賀寿男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōga Toshio|qq=}} suggests that, like the {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umakaibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (horse breeders) and the {{ill|Ikaibe|ja|猪養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikaibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (boar hunters), members of the Toriyaube (鳥養部) likely bore facial tattoos (黥, mesaki) as part of their occupation. Although the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] mentions punitive tattoos, he argues that tattooing originally served a protective, magical purpose against animal attacks. He further suggests that the punitive interpretation arose later, when tattooing became associated with criminal punishment.<ref>「[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/hitori/irezumi.htm 入墨についての雑考]」『古樹紀之房間』、2010th year 。</ref>
During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]], {{ill|Musa no Suguri Ao|ja|身狭村主青|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musa no Suguri Ao|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 身狭青|1=Musa no Suguri Ao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52320012}} and his men transported imported [[:en:Domestic goose|geese]] from the {{ill|Southern Dynasties (China)|ja|南朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Dynasties (China)|1=Southern Dynasties|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1206988}} to [[:en:Tsukushi Province|Tsukushi Province]]. However, a dog belonging to either {{ill|Minuma no Kimi|ja|水沼県主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minuma no Kimi|1=Minuma no Kimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548885}} or, in another account, {{ill|Mine no Agatanushi Numaro|ja|嶺県主泥麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mine no Agatanushi Numaro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, killed one of the birds. In a panic, the dog’s owner offered ten white swans and several "Toriyaubito" (養鳥人, birdkeepers) as compensation.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 9月4日条</ref>
The emperor settled these birdkeepers in Karumura and Iwaremura.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 10月7日条</ref>
Later, when a bird raised by the "Tori no Tsukasa" (鳥官, the imperial birdkeepers) was killed by a pet dog in {{ill|Uda District|ja|宇陀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uda District|1=Uda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11449912}}, the emperor punished the dog’s owner by tattooing his face and assigning him to the Toriyaube. The same punishment was imposed on men from [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]] and [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] who were serving in forced labor and had spoken out against the emperor’s decision.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇11th year 10月条</ref>
{{ill|Ken'ichi Tanigawa|lt=Ken'ichi Tanigawa|lt=Tanigawa Ken'ichi|ja|谷川健一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanigawa Ken'ichi|qq=}} has noted a deep connection between metal refining and bird-related myths. He argues that the name "Homutsuwake" implies a birth in fire and that "Yue" (湯坐) secretly carries the meaning of "molten metal." He also points to links between {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河板挙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawatana|qq=}} and {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, suggesting that the [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]], who worshipped the thunder god {{ill|Nigi-hayahinomikoto|ja|ニギハヤヒ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigi-hayahinomikoto|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}, may have overseen the Toriyaube.<ref>『白鳥伝説』(上)p215 - p238、集英社文庫</ref>
The [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] also records the story of [[:en:Totoribe no Yorozu|Totori-be no Yorozu]], a retainer of the [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]], who defended the clan’s estate during the [[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Battle against Mononobe no Moriya]] in the second year of [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]] (587). After their defeat, he fled but resisted capture by the imperial forces.<ref>『日本書紀』[[:en:Emperor Sushun|Emperor Sushun]]即位前紀（用明天皇2nd year 7月条）</ref>

== References == * ''Kadokawa Nihonshi Jiten'' (2nd Edition), p. 699, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, 1974, Kadokawa Shoten * ''Kojiki'' (Complete Annotated Japanese Classics, Vol. 1), Shogakukan, 1983 * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volumes 2-4), Iwanami Bunko, 1994-1995 * ''The Legend of the White Bird'' (Volume 1), Kenichi Tanigawa, Shueisha Bunko, 1988 == See Also == *[[:en:Bemin|Be System]] - {{ill|Inukaibe|ja|犬養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inukaibe|qq=}} / {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umakaibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} *[[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]] - [[:en:Tottori (city)|Tottori City]] *{{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Shrine|1=Totori no kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48760952}} *[[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]] *[[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]] *[[:en:Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]]

{{wikidata link|Q55525380}} [[Category:Ancient Japan]] [[Category:Bumin]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:History of Tottori]] [[Category:History of Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鳥養部]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鳥養部]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鳥養部]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q55525380]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Torikaibe''' (鳥養部) was a professional group (品部) in Japan before the [[Taika Reform]] that was responsible for raising and breeding birds captured by the [[Toribe]] (鳥取部) at the imperial court's request. It was also written as "Torigai-be" or "Toriame-be" (鳥飼部・鳥甘部). This article also covers the [[Toribe]] (ととりべ), another occupational group that specialized in capturing waterfowl for the court.
== Overview (概要) ==
The '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) oversaw the '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部), a specialized occupational group (*be*) responsible for capturing ornamental waterfowl. Meanwhile, the '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部) was likely responsible for the care and breeding of these captured birds.
In the central administration, high-Ranking {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomonomiyatsuko|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} were given the title of '''"Zō"''' (造), later changed to '''"Muraji"''' (連), while lower-Ranking officials held titles such as '''"Omi"''' (臣), '''"Atonae"''' (直), '''"Zō"''' (造), and '''"Kubi"''' (首).<ref>『日本書紀』(二)補注p349、岩波文庫、1994th year </ref>
The descendants of {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河桁命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawata|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天湯河板挙}} were known as '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] and '''Totori Muraji''' (鳥取連) in the [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]. His son, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, was also identified as the ancestor of the '''Totori Muraji''' in the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]].
The '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部) is documented in historical records from [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]], [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]], {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and [[:en:Bitchū Province|Bitchū Province]]. The name also appears in the form of '''villages, postal relay stations (eki), and shrines''' in various regions, including [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]], [[:en:Izumi Province|Izumi Province]], {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Etchū Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]], {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Bizen Province|Bizen Province]], [[:en:Higo Province|Higo Province]], [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]], and [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]].
The '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部, also written 鳥甘部) appears exclusively in historical records from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]. Based on place names, they were likely concentrated in the '''Karumura and Iware''' (磐余) regions, with additional settlements in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], [[:en:Chikuzen Province|Chikuzen Province]], and [[:en:Chikugo Province|Chikugo Province]], where there were villages and districts named '''Torikabe''' (鳥養村, 鳥養郷).
== Origins (由来) ==
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], {{ill|Homutsuwake no Mikoto|lt=Homutsuwake no Mikoto|lt=Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi|ja|誉津別命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi|qq=}} (ほむちわけ の おおきみ) did not speak for a long time even after reaching adulthood, possibly due to the shock of his mother’s death, [[:en:Saho-hime|Saho-hime no Mikoto]]. One day, while on a boat excursion, he heard the voice of a {{ill|Crested ibis|ja|鵠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crested ibis|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハクチョウ|1=swan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q34384}} (くぐい) flying high in the sky and moved his jaw, uttering his first words.
His father, the emperor, dispatched {{ill|Yamanobe no Ōtaka|ja|山辺大鶙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Ōtaka|1=Yamabe no Ōtaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52319925}} to chase the ibis across [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]], [[:en:Harima Province|Harima Province]], {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], [[:en:Tajima Province|Tajima Province]], [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]], [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]], [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]], and [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], until finally capturing it at Wanaumi no Minato (和那美之水門) in the [[:en:Koshi Province (Japan)|Koshi region]] using a trap net. However, the prince still could not speak.
Later, in a dream, the emperor was visited by {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, who instructed him to build a shrine. It was decided that Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi should make a pilgrimage to [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], with his attendant selected by divination. {{ill|Aketatsu no Ōkimi|ja|曙立王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aketatsu no Ōkimi|1=Aketatsu no Ōkimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11515056}} was chosen and, to prove the divine message, he performed a [[:en:Ukehi|ukei]] by reviving a {{ill|heron|ja|鷺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/heron|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サギ|1=heron|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3259077}} and an {{ill|evergreen oak|ja|樫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/evergreen oak|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カシ|1=Cyclobalanopsis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q796117}} tree.

There, the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} prepared a temporary shrine decorated with fresh green leaves and constructed an artificial hill. As they prepared to dine by the lower reaches of the [[:en:Hii River|Hii River]], the prince finally spoke, asking about the artificial hill. This event led to the establishment of the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottoribe|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鳥養部|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, {{ill|Torikamebe|ja|鳥甘部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Torikamebe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Homujibe|ja|品遅部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homujibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ōyu-e|ja|大湯坐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyu-e|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Wakayu-e|ja|若湯坐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayu-e|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『古事記』中巻、垂仁天皇条より</ref>
According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], {{ill|Prince Homutsuwake|ja|誉津別皇子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Homutsuwake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, son of Emperor Suinin, did not speak even after growing a beard and continued to cry like a child. One day, he saw a crested ibis and uttered his first words: "What is that?" The emperor then sent {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河板挙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawatana|qq=}} to chase the ibis to Izumo (or Tajima, according to another account), where he captured it. As a result, the prince was able to speak.
The emperor then granted Ame-no-Yukawatana the surname "Tottori no Miyatsuko" (鳥取造), meaning "head of the bird-catchers," and established the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottoribe|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鳥養部|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, {{ill|Toriyaube|ja|鳥養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toriyaube|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, and {{ill|Homutsube|ja|誉津部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homutsube|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『日本書紀』垂仁天皇23rd year 10月8日条、11月2日条</ref>

Although the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] mentions punitive tattoos, he argues that tattooing originally served a protective, magical purpose against animal attacks. He further suggests that the punitive interpretation arose later, when tattooing became associated with criminal punishment.<ref>「[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/hitori/irezumi.htm 入墨についての雑考]」『古樹紀之房間』、2010th year 。</ref>
During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]], {{ill|Musa no Suguri Ao|ja|身狭村主青|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musa no Suguri Ao|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 身狭青|1=Musa no Suguri Ao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52320012}} and his men transported imported [[:en:Domestic goose|geese]] from the {{ill|Southern Dynasties (China)|ja|南朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Dynasties (China)|1=Southern Dynasties|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1206988}} to [[:en:Tsukushi Province|Tsukushi Province]]. However, a dog belonging to either {{ill|Minuma no Kimi|ja|水沼県主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minuma no Kimi|1=Minuma no Kimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548885}} or, in another account, {{ill|Mine no Agatanushi Numaro|ja|嶺県主泥麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mine no Agatanushi Numaro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, killed one of the birds. In a panic, the dog’s owner offered ten white swans and several "Toriyaubito" (養鳥人, birdkeepers) as compensation.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 9月4日条</ref>
The emperor settled these birdkeepers in Karumura and Iwaremura.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 10月7日条</ref>
Later, when a bird raised by the "Tori no Tsukasa" (鳥官, the imperial birdkeepers) was killed by a pet dog in {{ill|Uda District|ja|宇陀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uda District|1=Uda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11449912}}, the emperor punished the dog’s owner by tattooing his face and assigning him to the Toriyaube. The same punishment was imposed on men from [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]] and [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] who were serving in forced labor and had spoken out against the emperor’s decision.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇11th year 10月条</ref>
{{ill|Ken'ichi Tanigawa|lt=Ken'ichi Tanigawa|lt=Tanigawa Ken'ichi|ja|谷川健一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanigawa Ken'ichi|qq=}} has noted a deep connection between metal refining and bird-related myths. He argues that the name "Homutsuwake" implies a birth in fire and that "Yue" (湯坐) secretly carries the meaning of "molten metal." He also points to links between {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河板挙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawatana|qq=}} and {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, suggesting that the [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]], who worshipped the thunder god {{ill|Nigi-hayahinomikoto|ja|ニギハヤヒ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigi-hayahinomikoto|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}, may have overseen the Toriyaube.<ref>『白鳥伝説』(上)p215 - p238、集英社文庫</ref>
The [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] also records the story of [[:en:Totoribe no Yorozu|Totori-be no Yorozu]], a retainer of the [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]], who defended the clan’s estate during the [[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Battle against Mononobe no Moriya]] in the second year of [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]] (587). After their defeat, he fled but resisted capture by the imperial forces.<ref>『日本書紀』[[:en:Emperor Sushun|Emperor Sushun]]即位前紀（用明天皇2nd year 7月条）</ref>
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Kadokawa Nihonshi Jiten'' (2nd Edition), p. 699, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, 1974, Kadokawa Shoten * ''Kojiki'' (Complete Annotated Japanese Classics, Vol. 1), Shogakukan, 1983 * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volumes 2-4), Iwanami Bunko, 1994-1995 * ''The Legend of the White Bird'' (Volume 1), Kenichi Tanigawa, Shueisha Bunko, 1988 == See Also == *[[:en:Bemin|Be System]] - {{ill|Inukaibe|ja|犬養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inukaibe|qq=}} / {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umakaibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} *[[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]] - [[:en:Tottori (city)|Tottori City]] *{{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Shrine|1=Totori no kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48760952}} *[[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]] *[[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]] *[[:en:Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]]

{{wikidata link|Q55525380}} [[Category:Ancient Japan]] [[Category:Bumin]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:History of Tottori]] [[Category:History of Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Pages with conflicting qids]] to [[Category:Qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鳥養部]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鳥養部]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鳥養部]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q55525380]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Torikaibe''' (鳥養部) was a professional group (品部) in Japan before the [[Taika Reform]] that was responsible for raising and breeding birds captured by the [[Toribe]] (鳥取部) at the imperial court's request. It was also written as "Torigai-be" or "Toriame-be" (鳥飼部・鳥甘部). This article also covers the [[Toribe]] (ととりべ), another occupational group that specialized in capturing waterfowl for the court.
== Overview (概要) ==
The '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) oversaw the '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部), a specialized occupational group (*be*) responsible for capturing ornamental waterfowl. Meanwhile, the '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部) was likely responsible for the care and breeding of these captured birds.
In the central administration, high-Ranking {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomonomiyatsuko|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} were given the title of '''"Zō"''' (造), later changed to '''"Muraji"''' (連), while lower-Ranking officials held titles such as '''"Omi"''' (臣), '''"Atonae"''' (直), '''"Zō"''' (造), and '''"Kubi"''' (首).<ref>『日本書紀』(二)補注p349、岩波文庫、1994th year </ref>
The descendants of {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河桁命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawata|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天湯河板挙}} were known as '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] and '''Totori Muraji''' (鳥取連) in the [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]. His son, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, was also identified as the ancestor of the '''Totori Muraji''' in the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]].
The '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部) is documented in historical records from [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]], [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]], {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and [[:en:Bitchū Province|Bitchū Province]]. The name also appears in the form of '''villages, postal relay stations (eki), and shrines''' in various regions, including [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]], [[:en:Izumi Province|Izumi Province]], {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Etchū Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]], {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Bizen Province|Bizen Province]], [[:en:Higo Province|Higo Province]], [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]], and [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]].
The '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部, also written 鳥甘部) appears exclusively in historical records from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]. Based on place names, they were likely concentrated in the '''Karumura and Iware''' (磐余) regions, with additional settlements in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], [[:en:Chikuzen Province|Chikuzen Province]], and [[:en:Chikugo Province|Chikugo Province]], where there were villages and districts named '''Torikabe''' (鳥養村, 鳥養郷).
== Origins (由来) ==
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], {{ill|Homutsuwake no Mikoto|lt=Homutsuwake no Mikoto|lt=Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi|ja|誉津別命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi|qq=}} (ほむちわけ の おおきみ) did not speak for a long time even after reaching adulthood, possibly due to the shock of his mother’s death, [[:en:Saho-hime|Saho-hime no Mikoto]]. One day, while on a boat excursion, he heard the voice of a {{ill|Crested ibis|ja|鵠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crested ibis|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハクチョウ|1=swan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q34384}} (くぐい) flying high in the sky and moved his jaw, uttering his first words.
His father, the emperor, dispatched {{ill|Yamanobe no Ōtaka|ja|山辺大鶙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Ōtaka|1=Yamabe no Ōtaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52319925}} to chase the ibis across [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]], [[:en:Harima Province|Harima Province]], {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], [[:en:Tajima Province|Tajima Province]], [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]], [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]], [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]], and [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], until finally capturing it at Wanaumi no Minato (和那美之水門) in the [[:en:Koshi Province (Japan)|Koshi region]] using a trap net. However, the prince still could not speak.
Later, in a dream, the emperor was visited by {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, who instructed him to build a shrine. It was decided that Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi should make a pilgrimage to [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], with his attendant selected by divination. {{ill|Aketatsu no Ōkimi|ja|曙立王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aketatsu no Ōkimi|1=Aketatsu no Ōkimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11515056}} was chosen and, to prove the divine message, he performed a [[:en:Ukehi|ukei]] by reviving a {{ill|heron|ja|鷺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/heron|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サギ|1=heron|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3259077}} and an {{ill|evergreen oak|ja|樫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/evergreen oak|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カシ|1=Cyclobalanopsis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q796117}} tree.
Emperor Suinin, convinced, sent Aketatsu no Ōkimi along with his younger brother, {{ill|Unakami no Ōkimi|ja|菟上王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unakami no Ōkimi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, to accompany the prince to Izumo. There, the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} prepared a temporary shrine decorated with fresh green leaves and constructed an artificial hill. As they prepared to dine by the lower reaches of the [[:en:Hii River|Hii River]], the prince finally spoke, asking about the artificial hill. This event led to the establishment of the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottoribe|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鳥養部|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, {{ill|Torikamebe|ja|鳥甘部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Torikamebe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Homujibe|ja|品遅部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homujibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ōyu-e|ja|大湯坐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyu-e|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Wakayu-e|ja|若湯坐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayu-e|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『古事記』中巻、垂仁天皇条より</ref>
According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], {{ill|Prince Homutsuwake|ja|誉津別皇子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Homutsuwake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, son of Emperor Suinin, did not speak even after growing a beard and continued to cry like a child. One day, he saw a crested ibis and uttered his first words: "What is that?" The emperor then sent {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河板挙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawatana|qq=}} to chase the ibis to Izumo (or Tajima, according to another account), where he captured it. As a result, the prince was able to speak.
The emperor then granted Ame-no-Yukawatana the surname "Tottori no Miyatsuko" (鳥取造), meaning "head of the bird-catchers," and established the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottoribe|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鳥養部|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, {{ill|Toriyaube|ja|鳥養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toriyaube|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, and {{ill|Homutsube|ja|誉津部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homutsube|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『日本書紀』垂仁天皇23rd year 10月8日条、11月2日条</ref>
{{ill|Toshio Hōga|lt=Toshio Hōga|lt=Hōga Toshio|ja|宝賀寿男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōga Toshio|qq=}} suggests that, like the {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umakaibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (horse breeders) and the {{ill|Ikaibe|ja|猪養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikaibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (boar hunters), members of the Toriyaube (鳥養部) likely bore facial tattoos (黥, mesaki) as part of their occupation. Although the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] mentions punitive tattoos, he argues that tattooing originally served a protective, magical purpose against animal attacks. He further suggests that the punitive interpretation arose later, when tattooing became associated with criminal punishment.<ref>「[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/hitori/irezumi.htm 入墨についての雑考]」『古樹紀之房間』、2010th year 。</ref>
During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]], {{ill|Musa no Suguri Ao|ja|身狭村主青|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musa no Suguri Ao|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 身狭青|1=Musa no Suguri Ao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52320012}} and his men transported imported [[:en:Domestic goose|geese]] from the {{ill|Southern Dynasties (China)|ja|南朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Dynasties (China)|1=Southern Dynasties|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1206988}} to [[:en:Tsukushi Province|Tsukushi Province]]. However, a dog belonging to either {{ill|Minuma no Kimi|ja|水沼県主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minuma no Kimi|1=Minuma no Kimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548885}} or, in another account, {{ill|Mine no Agatanushi Numaro|ja|嶺県主泥麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mine no Agatanushi Numaro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, killed one of the birds. In a panic, the dog’s owner offered ten white swans and several "Toriyaubito" (養鳥人, birdkeepers) as compensation.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 9月4日条</ref>
The emperor settled these birdkeepers in Karumura and Iwaremura.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 10月7日条</ref>
Later, when a bird raised by the "Tori no Tsukasa" (鳥官, the imperial birdkeepers) was killed by a pet dog in {{ill|Uda District|ja|宇陀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uda District|1=Uda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11449912}}, the emperor punished the dog’s owner by tattooing his face and assigning him to the Toriyaube. The same punishment was imposed on men from [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]] and [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] who were serving in forced labor and had spoken out against the emperor’s decision.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇11th year 10月条</ref>
{{ill|Ken'ichi Tanigawa|lt=Ken'ichi Tanigawa|lt=Tanigawa Ken'ichi|ja|谷川健一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanigawa Ken'ichi|qq=}} has noted a deep connection between metal refining and bird-related myths. He argues that the name "Homutsuwake" implies a birth in fire and that "Yue" (湯坐) secretly carries the meaning of "molten metal." He also points to links between {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河板挙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawatana|qq=}} and {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, suggesting that the [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]], who worshipped the thunder god {{ill|Nigi-hayahinomikoto|ja|ニギハヤヒ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigi-hayahinomikoto|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}, may have overseen the Toriyaube.<ref>『白鳥伝説』(上)p215 - p238、集英社文庫</ref>
The [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] also records the story of [[:en:Totoribe no Yorozu|Totori-be no Yorozu]], a retainer of the [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]], who defended the clan’s estate during the [[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Battle against Mononobe no Moriya]] in the second year of [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]] (587). After their defeat, he fled but resisted capture by the imperial forces.<ref>『日本書紀』[[:en:Emperor Sushun|Emperor Sushun]]即位前紀（用明天皇2nd year 7月条）</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鳥養部]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鳥養部]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鳥養部]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q55525380]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Torikaibe''' (鳥養部) was a professional group (品部) in Japan before the [[Taika Reform]] that was responsible for raising and breeding birds captured by the [[Toribe]] (鳥取部) at the imperial court's request. It was also written as "Torigai-be" or "Toriame-be" (鳥飼部・鳥甘部). This article also covers the [[Toribe]] (ととりべ), another occupational group that specialized in capturing waterfowl for the court.
== Overview (概要) ==
The '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) oversaw the '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部), a specialized occupational group (*be*) responsible for capturing ornamental waterfowl. Meanwhile, the '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部) was likely responsible for the care and breeding of these captured birds.
In the central administration, high-Ranking {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomonomiyatsuko|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} were given the title of '''"Zō"''' (造), later changed to '''"Muraji"''' (連), while lower-Ranking officials held titles such as '''"Omi"''' (臣), '''"Atonae"''' (直), '''"Zō"''' (造), and '''"Kubi"''' (首).<ref>『日本書紀』(二)補注p349、岩波文庫、1994th year </ref>
The descendants of {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河桁命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawata|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天湯河板挙}} were known as '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] and '''Totori Muraji''' (鳥取連) in the [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]. His son, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, was also identified as the ancestor of the '''Totori Muraji''' in the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]].
The '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部) is documented in historical records from [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]], [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]], {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and [[:en:Bitchū Province|Bitchū Province]]. The name also appears in the form of '''villages, postal relay stations (eki), and shrines''' in various regions, including [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]], [[:en:Izumi Province|Izumi Province]], {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Etchū Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]], {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Bizen Province|Bizen Province]], [[:en:Higo Province|Higo Province]], [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]], and [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]].
The '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部, also written 鳥甘部) appears exclusively in historical records from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]. Based on place names, they were likely concentrated in the '''Karumura and Iware''' (磐余) regions, with additional settlements in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], [[:en:Chikuzen Province|Chikuzen Province]], and [[:en:Chikugo Province|Chikugo Province]], where there were villages and districts named '''Torikabe''' (鳥養村, 鳥養郷).
== Origins (由来) ==
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], {{ill|Homutsuwake no Mikoto|lt=Homutsuwake no Mikoto|lt=Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi|ja|誉津別命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi|qq=}} (ほむちわけ の おおきみ) did not speak for a long time even after reaching adulthood, possibly due to the shock of his mother’s death, [[:en:Saho-hime|Saho-hime no Mikoto]]. One day, while on a boat excursion, he heard the voice of a {{ill|Crested ibis|ja|鵠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crested ibis|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハクチョウ|1=swan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q34384}} (くぐい) flying high in the sky and moved his jaw, uttering his first words.
His father, the emperor, dispatched {{ill|Yamanobe no Ōtaka|ja|山辺大鶙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Ōtaka|1=Yamabe no Ōtaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52319925}} to chase the ibis across [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]], [[:en:Harima Province|Harima Province]], {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], [[:en:Tajima Province|Tajima Province]], [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]], [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]], [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]], and [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], until finally capturing it at Wanaumi no Minato (和那美之水門) in the [[:en:Koshi Province (Japan)|Koshi region]] using a trap net. However, the prince still could not speak.
Later, in a dream, the emperor was visited by {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, who instructed him to build a shrine. It was decided that Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi should make a pilgrimage to [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], with his attendant selected by divination. {{ill|Aketatsu no Ōkimi|ja|曙立王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aketatsu no Ōkimi|1=Aketatsu no Ōkimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11515056}} was chosen and, to prove the divine message, he performed a [[:en:Ukehi|ukei]] by reviving a {{ill|heron|ja|鷺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/heron|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サギ|1=heron|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3259077}} and an {{ill|evergreen oak|ja|樫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/evergreen oak|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カシ|1=Cyclobalanopsis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q796117}} tree.
Emperor Suinin, convinced, sent Aketatsu no Ōkimi along with his younger brother, {{ill|Unakami no Ōkimi|ja|菟上王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unakami no Ōkimi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, to accompany the prince to Izumo. There, the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} prepared a temporary shrine decorated with fresh green leaves and constructed an artificial hill. As they prepared to dine by the lower reaches of the [[:en:Hii River|Hii River]], the prince finally spoke, asking about the artificial hill. This event led to the establishment of the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottoribe|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鳥養部|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, {{ill|Torikamebe|ja|鳥甘部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Torikamebe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Homujibe|ja|品遅部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homujibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ōyu-e|ja|大湯坐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyu-e|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Wakayu-e|ja|若湯坐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayu-e|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『古事記』中巻、垂仁天皇条より</ref>
According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], {{ill|Prince Homutsuwake|ja|誉津別皇子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Homutsuwake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, son of Emperor Suinin, did not speak even after growing a beard and continued to cry like a child. One day, he saw a crested ibis and uttered his first words: "What is that?" The emperor then sent {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河板挙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawatana|qq=}} to chase the ibis to Izumo (or Tajima, according to another account), where he captured it. As a result, the prince was able to speak.
The emperor then granted Ame-no-Yukawatana the surname "Tottori no Miyatsuko" (鳥取造), meaning "head of the bird-catchers," and established the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottoribe|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鳥養部|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, {{ill|Toriyaube|ja|鳥養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toriyaube|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, and {{ill|Homutsube|ja|誉津部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homutsube|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『日本書紀』垂仁天皇23rd year 10月8日条、11月2日条</ref>
{{ill|Toshio Hōga|lt=Toshio Hōga|lt=Hōga Toshio|ja|宝賀寿男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōga Toshio|qq=}} suggests that, like the {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umakaibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (horse breeders) and the {{ill|Ikaibe|ja|猪養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikaibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (boar hunters), members of the Toriyaube (鳥養部) likely bore facial tattoos (黥, mesaki) as part of their occupation. Although the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] mentions punitive tattoos, he argues that tattooing originally served a protective, magical purpose against animal attacks. He further suggests that the punitive interpretation arose later, when tattooing became associated with criminal punishment.<ref>「[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/hitori/irezumi.htm 入墨についての雑考]」『古樹紀之房間』、2010th year 。</ref>
During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]], {{ill|Musa no Suguri Ao|ja|身狭村主青|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musa no Suguri Ao|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 身狭青|1=Musa no Suguri Ao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52320012}} and his men transported imported [[:en:Domestic goose|geese]] from the {{ill|Southern Dynasties (China)|ja|南朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Dynasties (China)|1=Southern Dynasties|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1206988}} to [[:en:Tsukushi Province|Tsukushi Province]]. However, a dog belonging to either {{ill|Minuma no Kimi|ja|水沼県主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minuma no Kimi|1=Minuma no Kimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548885}} or, in another account, {{ill|Mine no Agatanushi Numaro|ja|嶺県主泥麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mine no Agatanushi Numaro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, killed one of the birds. In a panic, the dog’s owner offered ten white swans and several "Toriyaubito" (養鳥人, birdkeepers) as compensation.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 9月4日条</ref>
The emperor settled these birdkeepers in Karumura and Iwaremura.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 10月7日条</ref>
Later, when a bird raised by the "Tori no Tsukasa" (鳥官, the imperial birdkeepers) was killed by a pet dog in {{ill|Uda District|ja|宇陀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uda District|1=Uda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11449912}}, the emperor punished the dog’s owner by tattooing his face and assigning him to the Toriyaube. The same punishment was imposed on men from [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]] and [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] who were serving in forced labor and had spoken out against the emperor’s decision.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇11th year 10月条</ref>
{{ill|Ken'ichi Tanigawa|lt=Ken'ichi Tanigawa|lt=Tanigawa Ken'ichi|ja|谷川健一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanigawa Ken'ichi|qq=}} has noted a deep connection between metal refining and bird-related myths. He argues that the name "Homutsuwake" implies a birth in fire and that "Yue" (湯坐) secretly carries the meaning of "molten metal." He also points to links between {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河板挙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawatana|qq=}} and {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, suggesting that the [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]], who worshipped the thunder god {{ill|Nigi-hayahinomikoto|ja|ニギハヤヒ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigi-hayahinomikoto|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}, may have overseen the Toriyaube.<ref>『白鳥伝説』(上)p215 - p238、集英社文庫</ref>
The [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] also records the story of [[:en:Totoribe no Yorozu|Totori-be no Yorozu]], a retainer of the [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]], who defended the clan’s estate during the [[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Battle against Mononobe no Moriya]] in the second year of [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]] (587). After their defeat, he fled but resisted capture by the imperial forces.<ref>『日本書紀』[[:en:Emperor Sushun|Emperor Sushun]]即位前紀（用明天皇2nd year 7月条）</ref>

== References == * ''Kadokawa Nihonshi Jiten'' (2nd Edition), p. 699, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, 1974, Kadokawa Shoten * ''Kojiki'' (Complete Annotated Japanese Classics, Vol. 1), Shogakukan, 1983 * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volumes 2-4), Iwanami Bunko, 1994-1995 * ''The Legend of the White Bird'' (Volume 1), Kenichi Tanigawa, Shueisha Bunko, 1988 == See Also == *[[:en:Bemin|Be System]] - {{ill|Inukaibe|ja|犬養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inukaibe|qq=}} / {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umakaibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} *[[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]] - [[:en:Tottori (city)|Tottori City]] *{{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Shrine|1=Totori no kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48760952}} *[[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]] *[[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]] *[[:en:Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]]

{{wikidata link|Q55525380}} [[Category:Ancient Japan]] [[Category:Bumin]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:History of Tottori]] [[Category:History of Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鳥養部]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鳥養部]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鳥養部]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q55525380]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Torikaibe''' (鳥養部) was a professional group (品部) in Japan before the [[Taika Reform]] that was responsible for raising and breeding birds captured by the [[Toribe]] (鳥取部) at the imperial court's request. It was also written as "Torigai-be" or "Toriame-be" (鳥飼部・鳥甘部). This article also covers the [[Toribe]] (ととりべ), another occupational group that specialized in capturing waterfowl for the court.
== Overview (概要) ==
The '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) oversaw the '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部), a specialized occupational group (*be*) responsible for capturing ornamental waterfowl. Meanwhile, the '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部) was likely responsible for the care and breeding of these captured birds.
In the central administration, high-Ranking {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomonomiyatsuko|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} were given the title of '''"Zō"''' (造), later changed to '''"Muraji"''' (連), while lower-Ranking officials held titles such as '''"Omi"''' (臣), '''"Atonae"''' (直), '''"Zō"''' (造), and '''"Kubi"''' (首).<ref>『日本書紀』(二)補注p349、岩波文庫、1994th year </ref>
The descendants of {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河桁命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawata|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天湯河板挙}} were known as '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] and '''Totori Muraji''' (鳥取連) in the [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]. His son, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, was also identified as the ancestor of the '''Totori Muraji''' in the [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]].
The '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部) is documented in historical records from [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]], [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]], {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and [[:en:Bitchū Province|Bitchū Province]]. The name also appears in the form of '''villages, postal relay stations (eki), and shrines''' in various regions, including [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]], [[:en:Izumi Province|Izumi Province]], {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Etchū Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]], {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Bizen Province|Bizen Province]], [[:en:Higo Province|Higo Province]], [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]], and [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]].
The '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部, also written 鳥甘部) appears exclusively in historical records from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]. Based on place names, they were likely concentrated in the '''Karumura and Iware''' (磐余) regions, with additional settlements in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]], [[:en:Chikuzen Province|Chikuzen Province]], and [[:en:Chikugo Province|Chikugo Province]], where there were villages and districts named '''Torikabe''' (鳥養村, 鳥養郷).
== Origins (由来) ==
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], {{ill|Homutsuwake no Mikoto|lt=Homutsuwake no Mikoto|lt=Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi|ja|誉津別命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi|qq=}} (ほむちわけ の おおきみ) did not speak for a long time even after reaching adulthood, possibly due to the shock of his mother’s death, [[:en:Saho-hime|Saho-hime no Mikoto]]. According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], {{ill|Prince Homutsuwake|ja|誉津別皇子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Homutsuwake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, son of Emperor Suinin, did not speak even after growing a beard and continued to cry like a child. In a panic, the dog’s owner offered ten white swans and several "Toriyaubito" (養鳥人, birdkeepers) as compensation.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 9月4日条</ref>
The emperor settled these birdkeepers in Karumura and Iwaremura.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 10月7日条</ref>
Later, when a bird raised by the "Tori no Tsukasa" (鳥官, the imperial birdkeepers) was killed by a pet dog in {{ill|Uda District|ja|宇陀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uda District|1=Uda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11449912}}, the emperor punished the dog’s owner by tattooing his face and assigning him to the Toriyaube. The same punishment was imposed on men from [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]] and [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] who were serving in forced labor and had spoken out against the emperor’s decision.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇11th year 10月条</ref>
{{ill|Ken'ichi Tanigawa|lt=Ken'ichi Tanigawa|lt=Tanigawa Ken'ichi|ja|谷川健一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanigawa Ken'ichi|qq=}} has noted a deep connection between metal refining and bird-related myths. He argues that the name "Homutsuwake" implies a birth in fire and that "Yue" (湯坐) secretly carries the meaning of "molten metal." He also points to links between {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河板挙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawatana|qq=}} and {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, suggesting that the [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]], who worshipped the thunder god {{ill|Nigi-hayahinomikoto|ja|ニギハヤヒ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigi-hayahinomikoto|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}, may have overseen the Toriyaube.<ref>『白鳥伝説』(上)p215 - p238、集英社文庫</ref>
The [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] also records the story of [[:en:Totoribe no Yorozu|Totori-be no Yorozu]], a retainer of the [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]], who defended the clan’s estate during the [[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Battle against Mononobe no Moriya]] in the second year of [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]] (587). After their defeat, he fled but resisted capture by the imperial forces.<ref>『日本書紀』[[:en:Emperor Sushun|Emperor Sushun]]即位前紀（用明天皇2nd year 7月条）</ref>
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Kadokawa Nihonshi Jiten'' (2nd Edition), p. 699, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, 1974, Kadokawa Shoten * ''Kojiki'' (Complete Annotated Japanese Classics, Vol. 1), Shogakukan, 1983 * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volumes 2-4), Iwanami Bunko, 1994-1995 * ''The Legend of the White Bird'' (Volume 1), Kenichi Tanigawa, Shueisha Bunko, 1988 == See Also == *[[:en:Bemin|Be System]] - {{ill|Inukaibe|ja|犬養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inukaibe|qq=}} / {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umakaibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} *[[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]] - [[:en:Tottori (city)|Tottori City]] *{{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Shrine|1=Totori no kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48760952}} *[[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]] *[[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]] *[[:en:Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]]
