revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鳥養部]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鳥養部]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鳥養部]]
'''Torikaibe''' (鳥養部) was a professional group (品部) in Japan before the [[Taika Reform]] that was responsible for raising and breeding birds captured by the [[Toribe]] (鳥取部) at the imperial court's request. It was also written as "Torigai-be" or "Toriame-be" (鳥飼部・鳥甘部). This article also covers the [[Toribe]] (ととりべ), another occupational group that specialized in capturing waterfowl for the court.
== Overview (概要) ==
The '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) oversaw the '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部), a specialized occupational group (*be*) responsible for capturing ornamental waterfowl. Meanwhile, the '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部) was likely responsible for the care and breeding of these captured birds.
In the central administration, high-Ranking {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomonomiyatsuko|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} were given the title of '''"Zō"''' (造), later changed to '''"Muraji"''' (連), while lower-Ranking officials held titles such as '''"Omi"''' (臣), '''"Atonae"''' (直), '''"Zō"''' (造), and '''"Kubi"''' (首).<ref>『日本書紀』(二)補注p349、岩波文庫、1994th year </ref>
The descendants of {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河桁命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawata|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天湯河板挙}} were known as '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} and '''Totori Muraji''' (鳥取連) in the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}. His son, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, was also identified as the ancestor of the '''Totori Muraji''' in the {{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|WD=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi|1=Kujiki}}.
The '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部) is documented in historical records from {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}}, {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino Province|1=Mino Province}}, {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Bitchū Province|en|Bitchū Province|WD=Q1047514|lt=Bitchū Province|1=Bitchū Province}}. The name also appears in the form of '''villages, postal relay stations (eki), and shrines''' in various regions, including {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province|1=Kawachi Province}}, {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|WD=Q1047117|lt=Izumi Province|1=Izumi Province}}, {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Etchū Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tango Province|en|Tango Province|WD=Q1054331|lt=Tango Province|1=Tango Province}}, {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Bizen Province|en|Bizen Province|WD=Q1053688|lt=Bizen Province|1=Bizen Province}}, {{ill|Higo Province|en|Higo Province|WD=Q259705|lt=Higo Province|1=Higo Province}}, {{ill|Shimōsa Province|en|Shimōsa Province|WD=Q1053695|lt=Shimōsa Province|1=Shimōsa Province}}, and {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province|1=Ise Province}}.
The '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部, also written 鳥甘部) appears exclusively in historical records from {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}}. Based on place names, they were likely concentrated in the '''Karumura and Iware''' (磐余) regions, with additional settlements in {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province|1=Settsu Province}}, {{ill|Chikuzen Province|en|Chikuzen Province|WD=Q1047664|lt=Chikuzen Province|1=Chikuzen Province}}, and {{ill|Chikugo Province|en|Chikugo Province|WD=Q864159|lt=Chikugo Province|1=Chikugo Province}}, where there were villages and districts named '''Torikabe''' (鳥養村, 鳥養郷).
== Origins (由来) ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}}, {{ill|Homutsuwake no Mikoto|lt=Homutsuwake no Mikoto|lt=Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi|ja|誉津別命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi|qq=}} (ほむちわけ の おおきみ) did not speak for a long time even after reaching adulthood, possibly due to the shock of his mother’s death, {{ill|Saho-hime|en|Saho-hime|WD=Q11571754|lt=Saho-hime no Mikoto|1=Saho-hime}}. One day, while on a boat excursion, he heard the voice of a {{ill|Crested ibis|ja|鵠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crested ibis|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハクチョウ|1=swan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q34384}} (くぐい) flying high in the sky and moved his jaw, uttering his first words.
His father, the emperor, dispatched {{ill|Yamanobe no Ōtaka|ja|山辺大鶙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Ōtaka|1=Yamabe no Ōtaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52319925}} to chase the ibis across {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}}, {{ill|Harima Province|en|Harima Province|WD=Q1047523|lt=Harima Province|1=Harima Province}}, {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province|1=Tanba Province}}, {{ill|Tajima Province|en|Tajima Province|WD=Q1053727|lt=Tajima Province|1=Tajima Province}}, {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}}, {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino Province|1=Mino Province}}, {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province|1=Owari Province}}, and {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province|1=Shinano Province}}, until finally capturing it at Wanaumi no Minato (和那美之水門) in the {{ill|Koshi Province (Japan)|en|Koshi Province (Japan)|WD=Q1351611|lt=Koshi region|1=Koshi Province (Japan)}} using a trap net. However, the prince still could not speak.
Later, in a dream, the emperor was visited by {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, who instructed him to build a shrine. It was decided that Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi should make a pilgrimage to {{ill|Izumo-taisha|en|Izumo-taisha|WD=Q696362|lt=Izumo Taisha|1=Izumo-taisha}}, with his attendant selected by divination. {{ill|Aketatsu no Ōkimi|ja|曙立王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aketatsu no Ōkimi|1=Aketatsu no Ōkimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11515056}} was chosen and, to prove the divine message, he performed a {{ill|Ukehi|en|Ukehi|WD=Q11260316|lt=ukei|1=Ukehi}} by reviving a {{ill|heron|ja|鷺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/heron|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サギ|1=heron|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3259077}} and an {{ill|evergreen oak|ja|樫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/evergreen oak|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カシ|1=Cyclobalanopsis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q796117}} tree.
Emperor Suinin, convinced, sent Aketatsu no Ōkimi along with his younger brother, {{ill|Unakami no Ōkimi|ja|菟上王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unakami no Ōkimi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, to accompany the prince to Izumo. There, the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} prepared a temporary shrine decorated with fresh green leaves and constructed an artificial hill. As they prepared to dine by the lower reaches of the {{ill|Hii River|en|Hii River|WD=Q11500714|lt=Hii River|1=Hii River}}, the prince finally spoke, asking about the artificial hill. This event led to the establishment of the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottoribe|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鳥養部|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, {{ill|Torikamebe|ja|鳥甘部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Torikamebe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Homujibe|ja|品遅部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homujibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ōyu-e|ja|大湯坐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyu-e|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Wakayu-e|ja|若湯坐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayu-e|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『古事記』中巻、垂仁天皇条より</ref>
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, {{ill|Prince Homutsuwake|ja|誉津別皇子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Homutsuwake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, son of Emperor Suinin, did not speak even after growing a beard and continued to cry like a child. One day, he saw a crested ibis and uttered his first words: "What is that?" The emperor then sent {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河板挙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawatana|qq=}} to chase the ibis to Izumo (or Tajima, according to another account), where he captured it. As a result, the prince was able to speak.
The emperor then granted Ame-no-Yukawatana the surname "Tottori no Miyatsuko" (鳥取造), meaning "head of the bird-catchers," and established the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottoribe|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鳥養部|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, {{ill|Toriyaube|ja|鳥養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toriyaube|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, and {{ill|Homutsube|ja|誉津部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homutsube|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『日本書紀』垂仁天皇23rd year 10月8日条、11月2日条</ref>
{{ill|Toshio Hōga|lt=Toshio Hōga|lt=Hōga Toshio|ja|宝賀寿男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōga Toshio|qq=}} suggests that, like the {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umakaibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (horse breeders) and the {{ill|Ikaibe|ja|猪養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikaibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (boar hunters), members of the Toriyaube (鳥養部) likely bore facial tattoos (黥, mesaki) as part of their occupation. Although the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} mentions punitive tattoos, he argues that tattooing originally served a protective, magical purpose against animal attacks. He further suggests that the punitive interpretation arose later, when tattooing became associated with criminal punishment.<ref>「[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/hitori/irezumi.htm 入墨についての雑考]」『古樹紀之房間』、2010th year 。</ref>
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}}, {{ill|Musa no Suguri Ao|ja|身狭村主青|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musa no Suguri Ao|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 身狭青|1=Q52320012|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52320012}} and his men transported imported {{ill|Domestic goose|en|Domestic goose|WD=Q255503|lt=geese|1=Domestic goose}} from the {{ill|Southern Dynasties (China)|ja|南朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Dynasties (China)|1=Southern Dynasties|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1206988}} to {{ill|Tsukushi Province|en|Tsukushi Province|WD=Q1206461|lt=Tsukushi Province|1=Tsukushi Province}}. However, a dog belonging to either {{ill|Minuma no Kimi|ja|水沼県主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minuma no Kimi|1=Q11548885|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548885}} or, in another account, {{ill|Mine no Agatanushi Numaro|ja|嶺県主泥麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mine no Agatanushi Numaro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, killed one of the birds. In a panic, the dog’s owner offered ten white swans and several "Toriyaubito" (養鳥人, birdkeepers) as compensation.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 9月4日条</ref>
The emperor settled these birdkeepers in Karumura and Iwaremura.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 10月7日条</ref>
Later, when a bird raised by the "Tori no Tsukasa" (鳥官, the imperial birdkeepers) was killed by a pet dog in {{ill|Uda District|ja|宇陀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uda District|1=Uda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11449912}}, the emperor punished the dog’s owner by tattooing his face and assigning him to the Toriyaube. The same punishment was imposed on men from {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province|1=Shinano Province}} and {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}} who were serving in forced labor and had spoken out against the emperor’s decision.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇11th year 10月条</ref>
{{ill|Ken'ichi Tanigawa|lt=Ken'ichi Tanigawa|lt=Tanigawa Ken'ichi|ja|谷川健一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanigawa Ken'ichi|qq=}} has noted a deep connection between metal refining and bird-related myths. He argues that the name "Homutsuwake" implies a birth in fire and that "Yue" (湯坐) secretly carries the meaning of "molten metal." He also points to links between {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河板挙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawatana|qq=}} and {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, suggesting that the {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan|1=Mononobe clan}}, who worshipped the thunder god {{ill|Nigi-hayahinomikoto|ja|ニギハヤヒ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigi-hayahinomikoto|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}, may have overseen the Toriyaube.<ref>『白鳥伝説』(上)p215 - p238、集英社文庫</ref>
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} also records the story of {{ill|Totoribe no Yorozu|en|Totoribe no Yorozu|WD=Q11498122|lt=Totori-be no Yorozu|1=Totoribe no Yorozu}}, a retainer of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan|1=Mononobe clan}}, who defended the clan’s estate during the {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|en|Mononobe no Moriya|WD=Q2424736|lt=Battle against Mononobe no Moriya|1=Mononobe no Moriya}} in the second year of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|en|Emperor Yōmei|WD=Q349281|lt=Emperor Yōmei|1=Emperor Yōmei}} (587). After their defeat, he fled but resisted capture by the imperial forces.<ref>『日本書紀』{{ill|Emperor Sushun|en|Emperor Sushun|WD=Q349238|lt=Emperor Sushun|1=Emperor Sushun}}即位前紀（用明天皇2nd year 7月条）</ref>
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Kadokawa Nihonshi Jiten'' (2nd Edition), p. 699, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, 1974, Kadokawa Shoten * ''Kojiki'' (Complete Annotated Japanese Classics, Vol. 1), Shogakukan, 1983 * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volumes 2-4), Iwanami Bunko, 1994-1995 * ''The Legend of the White Bird'' (Volume 1), Kenichi Tanigawa, Shueisha Bunko, 1988 == See Also == *{{ill|Bemin|en|Bemin|WD=Q2892644|lt=Be System|1=Bemin}} - {{ill|Inukaibe|ja|犬養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inukaibe|qq=}} / {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umakaibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} *{{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture|1=Tottori Prefecture}} - {{ill|Tottori (city)|en|Tottori (city)|WD=Q200731|lt=Tottori City|1=Tottori (city)}} *{{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Shrine|1=Tottori-no-Kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48760952}} *{{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}} *{{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}} *{{ill|Soga–Mononobe conflict|en|Soga–Mononobe conflict|WD=Q534720|lt=Battle of Shigisan|1=Soga–Mononobe conflict}}

His son, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, was also identified as the ancestor of the '''Totori Muraji''' in the {{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|WD=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi|1=Kujiki}}.

The '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部, also written 鳥甘部) appears exclusively in historical records from {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}}. Based on place names, they were likely concentrated in the '''Karumura and Iware''' (磐余) regions, with additional settlements in {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province|1=Settsu Province}}, {{ill|Chikuzen Province|en|Chikuzen Province|WD=Q1047664|lt=Chikuzen Province|1=Chikuzen Province}}, and {{ill|Chikugo Province|en|Chikugo Province|WD=Q864159|lt=Chikugo Province|1=Chikugo Province}}, where there were villages and districts named '''Torikabe''' (鳥養村, 鳥養郷).
== Origins (由来) ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}}, {{ill|Homutsuwake no Mikoto|lt=Homutsuwake no Mikoto|lt=Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi|ja|誉津別命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi|qq=}} (ほむちわけ の おおきみ) did not speak for a long time even after reaching adulthood, possibly due to the shock of his mother’s death, {{ill|Saho-hime|en|Saho-hime|WD=Q11571754|lt=Saho-hime no Mikoto|1=Saho-hime}}. One day, while on a boat excursion, he heard the voice of a {{ill|Crested ibis|ja|鵠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crested ibis|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハクチョウ|1=swan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q34384}} (くぐい) flying high in the sky and moved his jaw, uttering his first words.
His father, the emperor, dispatched {{ill|Yamanobe no Ōtaka|ja|山辺大鶙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Ōtaka|1=Yamabe no Ōtaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52319925}} to chase the ibis across {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}}, {{ill|Harima Province|en|Harima Province|WD=Q1047523|lt=Harima Province|1=Harima Province}}, {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province|1=Tanba Province}}, {{ill|Tajima Province|en|Tajima Province|WD=Q1053727|lt=Tajima Province|1=Tajima Province}}, {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}}, {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino Province|1=Mino Province}}, {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province|1=Owari Province}}, and {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province|1=Shinano Province}}, until finally capturing it at Wanaumi no Minato (和那美之水門) in the {{ill|Koshi Province (Japan)|en|Koshi Province (Japan)|WD=Q1351611|lt=Koshi region|1=Koshi Province (Japan)}} using a trap net. However, the prince still could not speak.
Later, in a dream, the emperor was visited by {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, who instructed him to build a shrine. It was decided that Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi should make a pilgrimage to {{ill|Izumo-taisha|en|Izumo-taisha|WD=Q696362|lt=Izumo Taisha|1=Izumo-taisha}}, with his attendant selected by divination. {{ill|Aketatsu no Ōkimi|ja|曙立王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aketatsu no Ōkimi|1=Aketatsu no Ōkimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11515056}} was chosen and, to prove the divine message, he performed a {{ill|Ukehi|en|Ukehi|WD=Q11260316|lt=ukei|1=Ukehi}} by reviving a {{ill|heron|ja|鷺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/heron|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サギ|1=heron|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3259077}} and an {{ill|evergreen oak|ja|樫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/evergreen oak|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カシ|1=Cyclobalanopsis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q796117}} tree.
Emperor Suinin, convinced, sent Aketatsu no Ōkimi along with his younger brother, {{ill|Unakami no Ōkimi|ja|菟上王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unakami no Ōkimi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, to accompany the prince to Izumo. There, the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} prepared a temporary shrine decorated with fresh green leaves and constructed an artificial hill. As they prepared to dine by the lower reaches of the {{ill|Hii River|en|Hii River|WD=Q11500714|lt=Hii River|1=Hii River}}, the prince finally spoke, asking about the artificial hill. This event led to the establishment of the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottoribe|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鳥養部|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, {{ill|Torikamebe|ja|鳥甘部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Torikamebe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Homujibe|ja|品遅部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homujibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ōyu-e|ja|大湯坐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyu-e|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Wakayu-e|ja|若湯坐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayu-e|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『古事記』中巻、垂仁天皇条より</ref>
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, {{ill|Prince Homutsuwake|ja|誉津別皇子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Homutsuwake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, son of Emperor Suinin, did not speak even after growing a beard and continued to cry like a child. One day, he saw a crested ibis and uttered his first words: "What is that?" The emperor then sent {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河板挙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawatana|qq=}} to chase the ibis to Izumo (or Tajima, according to another account), where he captured it. As a result, the prince was able to speak.
The emperor then granted Ame-no-Yukawatana the surname "Tottori no Miyatsuko" (鳥取造), meaning "head of the bird-catchers," and established the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottoribe|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鳥養部|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, {{ill|Toriyaube|ja|鳥養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toriyaube|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, and {{ill|Homutsube|ja|誉津部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homutsube|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『日本書紀』垂仁天皇23rd year 10月8日条、11月2日条</ref>
{{ill|Toshio Hōga|lt=Toshio Hōga|lt=Hōga Toshio|ja|宝賀寿男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōga Toshio|qq=}} suggests that, like the {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umakaibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (horse breeders) and the {{ill|Ikaibe|ja|猪養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikaibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (boar hunters), members of the Toriyaube (鳥養部) likely bore facial tattoos (黥, mesaki) as part of their occupation. Although the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} mentions punitive tattoos, he argues that tattooing originally served a protective, magical purpose against animal attacks. He further suggests that the punitive interpretation arose later, when tattooing became associated with criminal punishment.<ref>「[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/hitori/irezumi.htm 入墨についての雑考]」『古樹紀之房間』、2010th year 。</ref>
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}}, {{ill|Musa no Suguri Ao|ja|身狭村主青|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musa no Suguri Ao|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 身狭青|1=Q52320012|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52320012}} and his men transported imported {{ill|Domestic goose|en|Domestic goose|WD=Q255503|lt=geese|1=Domestic goose}} from the {{ill|Southern Dynasties (China)|ja|南朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Dynasties (China)|1=Southern Dynasties|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1206988}} to {{ill|Tsukushi Province|en|Tsukushi Province|WD=Q1206461|lt=Tsukushi Province|1=Tsukushi Province}}. However, a dog belonging to either {{ill|Minuma no Kimi|ja|水沼県主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minuma no Kimi|1=Q11548885|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548885}} or, in another account, {{ill|Mine no Agatanushi Numaro|ja|嶺県主泥麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mine no Agatanushi Numaro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, killed one of the birds. In a panic, the dog’s owner offered ten white swans and several "Toriyaubito" (養鳥人, birdkeepers) as compensation.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 9月4日条</ref>
The emperor settled these birdkeepers in Karumura and Iwaremura.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 10月7日条</ref>
Later, when a bird raised by the "Tori no Tsukasa" (鳥官, the imperial birdkeepers) was killed by a pet dog in {{ill|Uda District|ja|宇陀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uda District|1=Uda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11449912}}, the emperor punished the dog’s owner by tattooing his face and assigning him to the Toriyaube. The same punishment was imposed on men from {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province|1=Shinano Province}} and {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}} who were serving in forced labor and had spoken out against the emperor’s decision.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇11th year 10月条</ref>
{{ill|Ken'ichi Tanigawa|lt=Ken'ichi Tanigawa|lt=Tanigawa Ken'ichi|ja|谷川健一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanigawa Ken'ichi|qq=}} has noted a deep connection between metal refining and bird-related myths. He argues that the name "Homutsuwake" implies a birth in fire and that "Yue" (湯坐) secretly carries the meaning of "molten metal." He also points to links between {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河板挙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawatana|qq=}} and {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, suggesting that the {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan|1=Mononobe clan}}, who worshipped the thunder god {{ill|Nigi-hayahinomikoto|ja|ニギハヤヒ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigi-hayahinomikoto|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}, may have overseen the Toriyaube.<ref>『白鳥伝説』(上)p215 - p238、集英社文庫</ref>
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} also records the story of {{ill|Totoribe no Yorozu|en|Totoribe no Yorozu|WD=Q11498122|lt=Totori-be no Yorozu|1=Totoribe no Yorozu}}, a retainer of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan|1=Mononobe clan}}, who defended the clan’s estate during the {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|en|Mononobe no Moriya|WD=Q2424736|lt=Battle against Mononobe no Moriya|1=Mononobe no Moriya}} in the second year of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|en|Emperor Yōmei|WD=Q349281|lt=Emperor Yōmei|1=Emperor Yōmei}} (587). After their defeat, he fled but resisted capture by the imperial forces.<ref>『日本書紀』{{ill|Emperor Sushun|en|Emperor Sushun|WD=Q349238|lt=Emperor Sushun|1=Emperor Sushun}}即位前紀（用明天皇2nd year 7月条）</ref>

== References == * ''Kadokawa Nihonshi Jiten'' (2nd Edition), p. 699, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, 1974, Kadokawa Shoten * ''Kojiki'' (Complete Annotated Japanese Classics, Vol. 1), Shogakukan, 1983 * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volumes 2-4), Iwanami Bunko, 1994-1995 * ''The Legend of the White Bird'' (Volume 1), Kenichi Tanigawa, Shueisha Bunko, 1988 == See Also == *{{ill|Bemin|en|Bemin|WD=Q2892644|lt=Be System|1=Bemin}} - {{ill|Inukaibe|ja|犬養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inukaibe|qq=}} / {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umakaibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} *{{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture|1=Tottori Prefecture}} - {{ill|Tottori (city)|en|Tottori (city)|WD=Q200731|lt=Tottori City|1=Tottori (city)}} *{{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Shrine|1=Tottori-no-Kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48760952}} *{{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}} *{{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}} *{{ill|Soga–Mononobe conflict|en|Soga–Mononobe conflict|WD=Q534720|lt=Battle of Shigisan|1=Soga–Mononobe conflict}}

{{wikidata link|Q55525380}} [[Category:Ancient Japan]] [[Category:Bumin]] [[Category:History of Tottori]] [[Category:History of Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鳥養部]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鳥養部]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鳥養部]]
'''Torikaibe''' (鳥養部) was a professional group (品部) in Japan before the [[Taika Reform]] that was responsible for raising and breeding birds captured by the [[Toribe]] (鳥取部) at the imperial court's request. It was also written as "Torigai-be" or "Toriame-be" (鳥飼部・鳥甘部). This article also covers the [[Toribe]] (ととりべ), another occupational group that specialized in capturing waterfowl for the court.
== Overview (概要) ==
The '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) oversaw the '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部), a specialized occupational group (*be*) responsible for capturing ornamental waterfowl. Meanwhile, the '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部) was likely responsible for the care and breeding of these captured birds.
In the central administration, high-Ranking {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomonomiyatsuko|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} were given the title of '''"Zō"''' (造), later changed to '''"Muraji"''' (連), while lower-Ranking officials held titles such as '''"Omi"''' (臣), '''"Atonae"''' (直), '''"Zō"''' (造), and '''"Kubi"''' (首).<ref>『日本書紀』(二)補注p349、岩波文庫、1994th year </ref>
The descendants of {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河桁命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawata|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天湯河板挙}} were known as '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} and '''Totori Muraji''' (鳥取連) in the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}. His son, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, was also identified as the ancestor of the '''Totori Muraji''' in the {{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|WD=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi|1=Kujiki}}.
The '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部) is documented in historical records from {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}}, {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino Province|1=Mino Province}}, {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Bitchū Province|en|Bitchū Province|WD=Q1047514|lt=Bitchū Province|1=Bitchū Province}}. The name also appears in the form of '''villages, postal relay stations (eki), and shrines''' in various regions, including {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province|1=Kawachi Province}}, {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|WD=Q1047117|lt=Izumi Province|1=Izumi Province}}, {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Etchū Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tango Province|en|Tango Province|WD=Q1054331|lt=Tango Province|1=Tango Province}}, {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Bizen Province|en|Bizen Province|WD=Q1053688|lt=Bizen Province|1=Bizen Province}}, {{ill|Higo Province|en|Higo Province|WD=Q259705|lt=Higo Province|1=Higo Province}}, {{ill|Shimōsa Province|en|Shimōsa Province|WD=Q1053695|lt=Shimōsa Province|1=Shimōsa Province}}, and {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province|1=Ise Province}}.
The '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部, also written 鳥甘部) appears exclusively in historical records from {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}}. Based on place names, they were likely concentrated in the '''Karumura and Iware''' (磐余) regions, with additional settlements in {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province|1=Settsu Province}}, {{ill|Chikuzen Province|en|Chikuzen Province|WD=Q1047664|lt=Chikuzen Province|1=Chikuzen Province}}, and {{ill|Chikugo Province|en|Chikugo Province|WD=Q864159|lt=Chikugo Province|1=Chikugo Province}}, where there were villages and districts named '''Torikabe''' (鳥養村, 鳥養郷).
== Origins (由来) ==

His father, the emperor, dispatched {{ill|Yamanobe no Ōtaka|ja|山辺大鶙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Ōtaka|1=Yamabe no Ōtaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52319925}} to chase the ibis across {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}}, {{ill|Harima Province|en|Harima Province|WD=Q1047523|lt=Harima Province|1=Harima Province}}, {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province|1=Tanba Province}}, {{ill|Tajima Province|en|Tajima Province|WD=Q1053727|lt=Tajima Province|1=Tajima Province}}, {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}}, {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino Province|1=Mino Province}}, {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province|1=Owari Province}}, and {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province|1=Shinano Province}}, until finally capturing it at Wanaumi no Minato (和那美之水門) in the {{ill|Koshi Province (Japan)|en|Koshi Province (Japan)|WD=Q1351611|lt=Koshi region|1=Koshi Province (Japan)}} using a trap net. However, the prince still could not speak.
Later, in a dream, the emperor was visited by {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, who instructed him to build a shrine. It was decided that Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi should make a pilgrimage to {{ill|Izumo-taisha|en|Izumo-taisha|WD=Q696362|lt=Izumo Taisha|1=Izumo-taisha}}, with his attendant selected by divination. {{ill|Aketatsu no Ōkimi|ja|曙立王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aketatsu no Ōkimi|1=Aketatsu no Ōkimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11515056}} was chosen and, to prove the divine message, he performed a {{ill|Ukehi|en|Ukehi|WD=Q11260316|lt=ukei|1=Ukehi}} by reviving a {{ill|heron|ja|鷺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/heron|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サギ|1=heron|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3259077}} and an {{ill|evergreen oak|ja|樫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/evergreen oak|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カシ|1=Cyclobalanopsis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q796117}} tree.
Emperor Suinin, convinced, sent Aketatsu no Ōkimi along with his younger brother, {{ill|Unakami no Ōkimi|ja|菟上王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unakami no Ōkimi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, to accompany the prince to Izumo. There, the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} prepared a temporary shrine decorated with fresh green leaves and constructed an artificial hill. As they prepared to dine by the lower reaches of the {{ill|Hii River|en|Hii River|WD=Q11500714|lt=Hii River|1=Hii River}}, the prince finally spoke, asking about the artificial hill. This event led to the establishment of the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottoribe|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鳥養部|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, {{ill|Torikamebe|ja|鳥甘部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Torikamebe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Homujibe|ja|品遅部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homujibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ōyu-e|ja|大湯坐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyu-e|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Wakayu-e|ja|若湯坐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayu-e|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『古事記』中巻、垂仁天皇条より</ref>
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, {{ill|Prince Homutsuwake|ja|誉津別皇子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Homutsuwake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, son of Emperor Suinin, did not speak even after growing a beard and continued to cry like a child. One day, he saw a crested ibis and uttered his first words: "What is that?" The emperor then sent {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河板挙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawatana|qq=}} to chase the ibis to Izumo (or Tajima, according to another account), where he captured it. As a result, the prince was able to speak.
The emperor then granted Ame-no-Yukawatana the surname "Tottori no Miyatsuko" (鳥取造), meaning "head of the bird-catchers," and established the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottoribe|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鳥養部|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, {{ill|Toriyaube|ja|鳥養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toriyaube|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, and {{ill|Homutsube|ja|誉津部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homutsube|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『日本書紀』垂仁天皇23rd year 10月8日条、11月2日条</ref>
{{ill|Toshio Hōga|lt=Toshio Hōga|lt=Hōga Toshio|ja|宝賀寿男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōga Toshio|qq=}} suggests that, like the {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umakaibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (horse breeders) and the {{ill|Ikaibe|ja|猪養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikaibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (boar hunters), members of the Toriyaube (鳥養部) likely bore facial tattoos (黥, mesaki) as part of their occupation. Although the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} mentions punitive tattoos, he argues that tattooing originally served a protective, magical purpose against animal attacks. He further suggests that the punitive interpretation arose later, when tattooing became associated with criminal punishment.<ref>「[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/hitori/irezumi.htm 入墨についての雑考]」『古樹紀之房間』、2010th year 。</ref>
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}}, {{ill|Musa no Suguri Ao|ja|身狭村主青|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musa no Suguri Ao|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 身狭青|1=Q52320012|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52320012}} and his men transported imported {{ill|Domestic goose|en|Domestic goose|WD=Q255503|lt=geese|1=Domestic goose}} from the {{ill|Southern Dynasties (China)|ja|南朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Dynasties (China)|1=Southern Dynasties|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1206988}} to {{ill|Tsukushi Province|en|Tsukushi Province|WD=Q1206461|lt=Tsukushi Province|1=Tsukushi Province}}. However, a dog belonging to either {{ill|Minuma no Kimi|ja|水沼県主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minuma no Kimi|1=Q11548885|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548885}} or, in another account, {{ill|Mine no Agatanushi Numaro|ja|嶺県主泥麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mine no Agatanushi Numaro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, killed one of the birds. In a panic, the dog’s owner offered ten white swans and several "Toriyaubito" (養鳥人, birdkeepers) as compensation.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 9月4日条</ref>
The emperor settled these birdkeepers in Karumura and Iwaremura.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 10月7日条</ref>
Later, when a bird raised by the "Tori no Tsukasa" (鳥官, the imperial birdkeepers) was killed by a pet dog in {{ill|Uda District|ja|宇陀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uda District|1=Uda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11449912}}, the emperor punished the dog’s owner by tattooing his face and assigning him to the Toriyaube. The same punishment was imposed on men from {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province|1=Shinano Province}} and {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}} who were serving in forced labor and had spoken out against the emperor’s decision.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇11th year 10月条</ref>
{{ill|Ken'ichi Tanigawa|lt=Ken'ichi Tanigawa|lt=Tanigawa Ken'ichi|ja|谷川健一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanigawa Ken'ichi|qq=}} has noted a deep connection between metal refining and bird-related myths. He argues that the name "Homutsuwake" implies a birth in fire and that "Yue" (湯坐) secretly carries the meaning of "molten metal." He also points to links between {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河板挙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawatana|qq=}} and {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, suggesting that the {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan|1=Mononobe clan}}, who worshipped the thunder god {{ill|Nigi-hayahinomikoto|ja|ニギハヤヒ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigi-hayahinomikoto|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}, may have overseen the Toriyaube.<ref>『白鳥伝説』(上)p215 - p238、集英社文庫</ref>
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} also records the story of {{ill|Totoribe no Yorozu|en|Totoribe no Yorozu|WD=Q11498122|lt=Totori-be no Yorozu|1=Totoribe no Yorozu}}, a retainer of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan|1=Mononobe clan}}, who defended the clan’s estate during the {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|en|Mononobe no Moriya|WD=Q2424736|lt=Battle against Mononobe no Moriya|1=Mononobe no Moriya}} in the second year of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|en|Emperor Yōmei|WD=Q349281|lt=Emperor Yōmei|1=Emperor Yōmei}} (587). After their defeat, he fled but resisted capture by the imperial forces.<ref>『日本書紀』{{ill|Emperor Sushun|en|Emperor Sushun|WD=Q349238|lt=Emperor Sushun|1=Emperor Sushun}}即位前紀（用明天皇2nd year 7月条）</ref>
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''Kadokawa Nihonshi Jiten'' (2nd Edition), p. 699, edited by Mitsuhisa Takayanagi and Rizo Takeuchi, 1974, Kadokawa Shoten * ''Kojiki'' (Complete Annotated Japanese Classics, Vol. 1), Shogakukan, 1983 * ''Nihon Shoki'' (Volumes 2-4), Iwanami Bunko, 1994-1995 * ''The Legend of the White Bird'' (Volume 1), Kenichi Tanigawa, Shueisha Bunko, 1988 == See Also == *{{ill|Bemin|en|Bemin|WD=Q2892644|lt=Be System|1=Bemin}} - {{ill|Inukaibe|ja|犬養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inukaibe|qq=}} / {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umakaibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} *{{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture|1=Tottori Prefecture}} - {{ill|Tottori (city)|en|Tottori (city)|WD=Q200731|lt=Tottori City|1=Tottori (city)}} *{{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Shrine|1=Tottori-no-Kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48760952}} *{{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}} *{{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}} *{{ill|Soga–Mononobe conflict|en|Soga–Mononobe conflict|WD=Q534720|lt=Battle of Shigisan|1=Soga–Mononobe conflict}}

revision.comment: Bot: strip HTML comments; consolidate 7 interlanguage link(s) into wikidata link template (has duplicate-language pairs) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/24926181992|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
'''Torikaibe''' (鳥養部) was a professional group (品部) in Japan before the [[Taika Reform]] that was responsible for raising and breeding birds captured by the [[Toribe]] (鳥取部) at the imperial court's request. It was also written as "Torigai-be" or "Toriame-be" (鳥飼部・鳥甘部). This article also covers the [[Toribe]] (ととりべ), another occupational group that specialized in capturing waterfowl for the court.
== Overview (概要) ==
The '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) oversaw the '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部), a specialized occupational group (*be*) responsible for capturing ornamental waterfowl. Meanwhile, the '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部) was likely responsible for the care and breeding of these captured birds.
In the central administration, high-Ranking {{ill|Tomonomiyatsuko|ja|伴造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomonomiyatsuko|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} were given the title of '''"Zō"''' (造), later changed to '''"Muraji"''' (連), while lower-Ranking officials held titles such as '''"Omi"''' (臣), '''"Atonae"''' (直), '''"Zō"''' (造), and '''"Kubi"''' (首).<ref>『日本書紀』(二)補注p349、岩波文庫、1994th year </ref>
The descendants of {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河桁命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawata|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天湯河板挙}} were known as '''Totori no Miyatsuko''' (鳥取造) in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} and '''Totori Muraji''' (鳥取連) in the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}. His son, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, was also identified as the ancestor of the '''Totori Muraji''' in the {{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|WD=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi|1=Kujiki}}.
The '''Totori-be''' (鳥取部) is documented in historical records from {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}}, {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino Province|1=Mino Province}}, {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Bitchū Province|en|Bitchū Province|WD=Q1047514|lt=Bitchū Province|1=Bitchū Province}}. The name also appears in the form of '''villages, postal relay stations (eki), and shrines''' in various regions, including {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province|1=Kawachi Province}}, {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|WD=Q1047117|lt=Izumi Province|1=Izumi Province}}, {{ill|Etchū Province|ja|Etchū Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Etchū Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tango Province|en|Tango Province|WD=Q1054331|lt=Tango Province|1=Tango Province}}, {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Bizen Province|en|Bizen Province|WD=Q1053688|lt=Bizen Province|1=Bizen Province}}, {{ill|Higo Province|en|Higo Province|WD=Q259705|lt=Higo Province|1=Higo Province}}, {{ill|Shimōsa Province|en|Shimōsa Province|WD=Q1053695|lt=Shimōsa Province|1=Shimōsa Province}}, and {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province|1=Ise Province}}.
The '''Torikabe''' (鳥養部, also written 鳥甘部) appears exclusively in historical records from {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}}. Based on place names, they were likely concentrated in the '''Karumura and Iware''' (磐余) regions, with additional settlements in {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province|1=Settsu Province}}, {{ill|Chikuzen Province|en|Chikuzen Province|WD=Q1047664|lt=Chikuzen Province|1=Chikuzen Province}}, and {{ill|Chikugo Province|en|Chikugo Province|WD=Q864159|lt=Chikugo Province|1=Chikugo Province}}, where there were villages and districts named '''Torikabe''' (鳥養村, 鳥養郷).
== Origins (由来) ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}}, {{ill|Homutsuwake no Mikoto|lt=Homutsuwake no Mikoto|lt=Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi|ja|誉津別命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi|qq=}} (ほむちわけ の おおきみ) did not speak for a long time even after reaching adulthood, possibly due to the shock of his mother’s death, {{ill|Saho-hime|en|Saho-hime|WD=Q11571754|lt=Saho-hime no Mikoto|1=Saho-hime}}. One day, while on a boat excursion, he heard the voice of a {{ill|Crested ibis|ja|鵠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Crested ibis|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハクチョウ|1=swan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q34384}} (くぐい) flying high in the sky and moved his jaw, uttering his first words.
His father, the emperor, dispatched {{ill|Yamanobe no Ōtaka|ja|山辺大鶙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Ōtaka|1=Yamabe no Ōtaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52319925}} to chase the ibis across {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}}, {{ill|Harima Province|en|Harima Province|WD=Q1047523|lt=Harima Province|1=Harima Province}}, {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province|1=Tanba Province}}, {{ill|Tajima Province|en|Tajima Province|WD=Q1053727|lt=Tajima Province|1=Tajima Province}}, {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}}, {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino Province|1=Mino Province}}, {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province|1=Owari Province}}, and {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province|1=Shinano Province}}, until finally capturing it at Wanaumi no Minato (和那美之水門) in the {{ill|Koshi Province (Japan)|en|Koshi Province (Japan)|WD=Q1351611|lt=Koshi region|1=Koshi Province (Japan)}} using a trap net. However, the prince still could not speak.
Later, in a dream, the emperor was visited by {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, who instructed him to build a shrine. It was decided that Honmuchi-wake no Ōkimi should make a pilgrimage to {{ill|Izumo-taisha|en|Izumo-taisha|WD=Q696362|lt=Izumo Taisha|1=Izumo-taisha}}, with his attendant selected by divination. {{ill|Aketatsu no Ōkimi|ja|曙立王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aketatsu no Ōkimi|1=Aketatsu no Ōkimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11515056}} was chosen and, to prove the divine message, he performed a {{ill|Ukehi|en|Ukehi|WD=Q11260316|lt=ukei|1=Ukehi}} by reviving a {{ill|heron|ja|鷺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/heron|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サギ|1=heron|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3259077}} and an {{ill|evergreen oak|ja|樫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/evergreen oak|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カシ|1=Cyclobalanopsis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q796117}} tree.
Emperor Suinin, convinced, sent Aketatsu no Ōkimi along with his younger brother, {{ill|Unakami no Ōkimi|ja|菟上王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unakami no Ōkimi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, to accompany the prince to Izumo. There, the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} prepared a temporary shrine decorated with fresh green leaves and constructed an artificial hill. As they prepared to dine by the lower reaches of the {{ill|Hii River|en|Hii River|WD=Q11500714|lt=Hii River|1=Hii River}}, the prince finally spoke, asking about the artificial hill. This event led to the establishment of the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottoribe|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鳥養部|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, {{ill|Torikamebe|ja|鳥甘部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Torikamebe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Homujibe|ja|品遅部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homujibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ōyu-e|ja|大湯坐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyu-e|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Wakayu-e|ja|若湯坐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayu-e|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『古事記』中巻、垂仁天皇条より</ref>
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, {{ill|Prince Homutsuwake|ja|誉津別皇子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Homutsuwake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, son of Emperor Suinin, did not speak even after growing a beard and continued to cry like a child. One day, he saw a crested ibis and uttered his first words: "What is that?" The emperor then sent {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河板挙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawatana|qq=}} to chase the ibis to Izumo (or Tajima, according to another account), where he captured it. As a result, the prince was able to speak.
The emperor then granted Ame-no-Yukawatana the surname "Tottori no Miyatsuko" (鳥取造), meaning "head of the bird-catchers," and established the clans {{ill|Tottoribe|ja|鳥取部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottoribe|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鳥養部|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, {{ill|Toriyaube|ja|鳥養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toriyaube|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55525380}}, and {{ill|Homutsube|ja|誉津部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homutsube|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>『日本書紀』垂仁天皇23rd year 10月8日条、11月2日条</ref>
{{ill|Toshio Hōga|lt=Toshio Hōga|lt=Hōga Toshio|ja|宝賀寿男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōga Toshio|qq=}} suggests that, like the {{ill|Umakaibe|ja|馬飼部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umakaibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (horse breeders) and the {{ill|Ikaibe|ja|猪養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikaibe|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (boar hunters), members of the Toriyaube (鳥養部) likely bore facial tattoos (黥, mesaki) as part of their occupation. Although the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} mentions punitive tattoos, he argues that tattooing originally served a protective, magical purpose against animal attacks. He further suggests that the punitive interpretation arose later, when tattooing became associated with criminal punishment.<ref>「[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/hitori/irezumi.htm 入墨についての雑考]」『古樹紀之房間』、2010th year 。</ref>
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}}, {{ill|Musa no Suguri Ao|ja|身狭村主青|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musa no Suguri Ao|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 身狭青|1=Q52320012|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52320012}} and his men transported imported {{ill|Domestic goose|en|Domestic goose|WD=Q255503|lt=geese|1=Domestic goose}} from the {{ill|Southern Dynasties (China)|ja|南朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Dynasties (China)|1=Southern Dynasties|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1206988}} to {{ill|Tsukushi Province|en|Tsukushi Province|WD=Q1206461|lt=Tsukushi Province|1=Tsukushi Province}}. However, a dog belonging to either {{ill|Minuma no Kimi|ja|水沼県主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minuma no Kimi|1=Q11548885|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548885}} or, in another account, {{ill|Mine no Agatanushi Numaro|ja|嶺県主泥麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mine no Agatanushi Numaro|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, killed one of the birds. In a panic, the dog’s owner offered ten white swans and several "Toriyaubito" (養鳥人, birdkeepers) as compensation.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 9月4日条</ref>
The emperor settled these birdkeepers in Karumura and Iwaremura.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇10th year 10月7日条</ref>
Later, when a bird raised by the "Tori no Tsukasa" (鳥官, the imperial birdkeepers) was killed by a pet dog in {{ill|Uda District|ja|宇陀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uda District|1=Uda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11449912}}, the emperor punished the dog’s owner by tattooing his face and assigning him to the Toriyaube. The same punishment was imposed on men from {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province|1=Shinano Province}} and {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}} who were serving in forced labor and had spoken out against the emperor’s decision.<ref>『日本書紀』雄略天皇11th year 10月条</ref>
{{ill|Ken'ichi Tanigawa|lt=Ken'ichi Tanigawa|lt=Tanigawa Ken'ichi|ja|谷川健一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanigawa Ken'ichi|qq=}} has noted a deep connection between metal refining and bird-related myths. He argues that the name "Homutsuwake" implies a birth in fire and that "Yue" (湯坐) secretly carries the meaning of "molten metal." He also points to links between {{ill|Ame-no-Yukawata|ja|天湯河板挙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Yukawatana|qq=}} and {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少彦根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, suggesting that the {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan|1=Mononobe clan}}, who worshipped the thunder god {{ill|Nigi-hayahinomikoto|ja|ニギハヤヒ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigi-hayahinomikoto|1=Nigihayahi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10949054}}, may have overseen the Toriyaube.<ref>『白鳥伝説』(上)p215 - p238、集英社文庫</ref>
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} also records the story of {{ill|Totoribe no Yorozu|en|Totoribe no Yorozu|WD=Q11498122|lt=Totori-be no Yorozu|1=Totoribe no Yorozu}}, a retainer of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan|1=Mononobe clan}}, who defended the clan’s estate during the {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|en|Mononobe no Moriya|WD=Q2424736|lt=Battle against Mononobe no Moriya|1=Mononobe no Moriya}} in the second year of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|en|Emperor Yōmei|WD=Q349281|lt=Emperor Yōmei|1=Emperor Yōmei}} (587). After their defeat, he fled but resisted capture by the imperial forces.<ref>『日本書紀』{{ill|Emperor Sushun|en|Emperor Sushun|WD=Q349238|lt=Emperor Sushun|1=Emperor Sushun}}即位前紀（用明天皇2nd year 7月条）</ref>
